After a first few months of (a lot of) patience and training, it's then blossomed into the most incredible remaining months where we’ve really seen Flash’s true heart and soul. This dog is phenomenal in more ways than one, and it’s so sad he’s now become part of the MT furniture, and that the rescue has now become his home.

So for Flash’s 1 year anniversary, I took him off site to the nearby country park for a lovely stroll, followed by a visit to McD’s for some chicken nuggets.

Flash was suuuuuuch a good boy on his walk. He took in all the new smells, and took the new environment in his stride. He was even approached by 3 off lead small dogs, and didn’t react at all. He trotted along the park, with the world at his feet and it was the most amazing thing to see.

He is an absolute darling in the car, so we made a swift visit to McD’s for a box of 20 nuggets to eat together in our on site flat.

It was so nice to see Flash in a home like environment, he jumped straight on the sofa, and wandered around inspecting every room. We sat on the floor together eating our nuggets, and he eventually rested on the raised bed and had a little snooze.

It was heartbreaking putting him back in his kennel, when he seemed so at peace and settled in the flat. I just wish there was someone out there who could see him for everything he is and more!!!

Our beautiful, bonkers Flash has learnt a new trick and we thought we would share.

Flash has also been enjoying the sunshine with lots of walks and plays in the yards this week.

Flash continues to work hard on growing his calmness around new people and he is doing ok. He has now been 10 months here at the rescue and has an increasing fan base amongst the staff but he of course longs for a home and people of his own.

When I first started working with him, I was apprehensive. He was a much larger dog than I was used to working with, and he would mouth you til his hearts content. Not because he is a nasty dog, or means any harm whatsoever, but simply because he has come from a home (just out of puppyhood) and he has become extremely anxious and unsettled being in a kennel environment. On top of that, he has been taught 0 manners which definitely didn’t help with the matter!!! I struggled to bond with him, I struggled to understand why he wouldn’t listen when being told, and why all he wanted to do was treat my arm like a toy…

However I found myself thinking, what has Flash been through in his life that was ever HIS fault? He didn’t ask to spend so much of his life in kennels. He never asked to be put in situations where his anxiety gets the better of him, it was never his decision to not be trained to behave normally. So I decided to buck up my ideas and realise he’s not as scary as he seems, and he’s probably just as anxious being around me as I was him. And from that moment on, our relationship has blossomed…

This boy is truly a superstar in every sense of the word. He is SUCH a goofball, and can have you in fits of laughter within seconds of being with him. His personality is infectious, and as soon as he is comfortable with you and realises you are no threat to him, he shines like the star he is. It’s gutting that Flash doesn’t allow every staff member to see all sides of him. The side that shows his softness, his sweetness, his kindness. But that just makes me so much more grateful that he feels comfortable enough around me to do so.

You can see from his pictures, Flash is muzzle trained. Not because he is an aggressive dog, not because he is a violent dog, it’s simply due to his anxiousness. On one hand, Flash is a super intelligent Poodle but on the other hand, he doesn’t really show it. His anxiety gets the better of him, and he puts himself in uncomfortable positions with new people & then panics whilst he’s in it. It is SUPER important that whoever adopts Flash’s main priority is keeping him safe, and keeping him from making decisions that he feels uncertain about. He wants to make friends with everyone, but also decides halfway through some interactions that he’s not to keen on the thought too!

Among (of course) all of the other precious angels I look after the rescue, Flash is one of the reasons I get excited to go to work. You shouldn’t really have favourites, but Flash does take the cake. I cherish every single moment I get with him. Our lovely strolls up the country lanes, our snuggles in the back of his kennel after a long day, our zoomies down our big play yard. Every single moment & memory I look forward to recreating every single day. Nothing tops being around this dog, nothing else matters when you’re with Flash.

If I was in a position to take him home & work with him through his quirks, I absolutely would, in heartbeat in fact. There is just something so special about him, and I truly envy whoever is fortunate to adopt this incredible young man. And for as long as Flash remains in kennels, I will advocate for him, make him feel safe, comfortable and most of all loved.

Flash has completely changed my mind about Poodles. I am absolutely in love with him. He might not be the sharpest boy in the bunch, but he more than makes up for it with his goofy charm and endless silliness.We are still working on his mouthing when he becomes overexcited. Progress is steady, and every small step forward feels like a real achievement. Flash loves his freedom. We take daily trips to the big yard where he runs around like the happiest whirlwind you have ever seen. He would really benefit from visits to private dog fields where he can safely zoom and explore to his heart’s content.Meeting new people can still be a little tricky for him, but we have a careful plan in place to help him do so safely and comfortably. For those of us who know Flash well, he shows a side many would not expect. Beneath his anxiousness and the need for a muzzle at times is the sweetest, softest boy who adores snuggles and head tickles.He loves to stay close, gently resting his head in your lap until he drifts off to sleep. At the end of each day, we share a quiet moment together, curled up side by side until his eyes close and he is peacefully dreaming.Flash truly is a special boy who deserves a very special home. He needs someone who can offer love, patience, and understanding. In return, he will give unwavering loyalty and companionship for life.

Flash is an absolute character, this boy is full of life and once he knows you he adores you but his introductions to new people always need to be slow as this sensitive boy can be easily overwhelmed.

Flash works well within a routine, he has an abundance of energy and a super inquisitive nature which can sometimes lead to him becoming quite lively, he will often revert to ‘mouthing’ and jumping up when he is uncomfortable or unsure.

Flash has a super inquisitive nature and loves to observe what’s going on at a distance. This boy can also be an absolute cuddle monster and adores a snuggle with his favourite people, and will fall asleep next to us when we have the opportunity to sit quietly with him. One of flash’s unique traits is his love of a pamper session! I have never known a dog who enjoys a bath and hairdryer the way he does and he becomes quite docile under the hair dryer whilst having a gentle brush.

Flash’s ideal home would be a quiet an experienced dog owning home (adults only) with humans that have the patience and time to help grow his confidence and who also understand the need to grow calmness in him. Flash has had a few dog friends at the rescue but his high spirted nature can be a bit much for some dogs and he does not seem to like male dogs, for this reason a home where he is the only pooch would suit him best.

Flash walks well on lead but can become very excited when he sees other dogs, walks in quiet areas would be perfect for him, he loves to be outdoors and often just enjoys sitting in the yard with me watching birds, but he will equally enjoy time spent just hanging out with his humans at home. Flash really is a wonderfully, lovable, giant puppy, looking for kind and understanding humans to provide him with the security and love he deserves.

Flash has had a brilliant few weeks. He celebrated his second Birthday and was treated with his first snow day, which he loved frolicking about in. Unfortunately, he did have to spend his birthday in kennels, but I made sure it was still a very special day for him. We hopped in the car, and I took him for a little walk before heading to the pet shop to pick out some birthday treats. He chose a dinosaur toy and a knuckle bone, which he thoroughly enjoyed. I also bought him some special birthday biscuits to keep him going through the week.

After that, I truly spoiled him, and we decided to go to Burger King for a burger! Flash was quite confused at first, but once he had a taste, he couldn’t get enough.

Flash truly is a special boy and will need a special home to match. He can be very wary of strangers and doesn’t always show clear signs if he’s uncomfortable. For this reason, he is muzzled when out on walks, which I make sure to do whenever we go out.

Flash will need an adult-only home with no visiting children. He cannot live with cats or any small animals

The stunning Flash had a little pamper today, which he absolutely loved. He was as good as gold whilst in the bath and sat very contentedly on the grooming table looking out the window enjoying the warmth of the blowers. I have never known a dog to enjoy bath time as much as he does, he becomes quite sleepy and cuddley, it’s the cutest thing.

After his wash and blow dry he got to open some Christmas treats out in the yard sent in from the lovely folks out there that support Many Tears, Flash particular enjoyed the food presents.

Flash really is the most wonderful boy once you get to know him, and we see improvement every day in his sometimes lively and skittish nature. This fantastic boy will make the most awesome and loyal of friend with the person or couple that are prepared to put the time and love into him he so deserves.

Today, Flash and I went on a little adventure to the local pet shop, finishing off with a much-needed pupcup! He was an absolute angel in the car—he hopped right in, buckled up, and sat calmly for the whole journey. I watched him in the mirror as he gazed out at the world passing by. When we arrived, we popped his muzzle on as a precaution since he can sometimes be wary of strangers. Flash wandered around the shop, taking in all the sights and smells, until he discovered the pick-and-mix treats—which of course we had to get. After choosing his goodies, he rummaged through the plushies and picked out a squeaky monkey to take home.

As we headed toward the till, another dog suddenly rushed around a corner and startled Flash, causing him to react. I managed to whisk him away with a distraction, but he became very on edge and tucked himself into me for comfort. A lovely lady then came around the opposite corner and, despite having seen his reaction, kindly stopped to say hello. She happened to be a previous Many Tears adopter, and her own pup is doing wonderfully. She chose to ignore Flash at first to give him space, and once he settled, he allowed her to gently stroke his head. After wishing us well, we carried on with our day.

We sat outside Costa in the sunshine, and Flash absolutely loved his pupcup, so much so that he managed to get it all over his nose.

I’m incredibly proud of Flash today. It was his first outing since arriving in kennels, and I could really see how scared he can be underneath it all, and how much he relies on his human for reassurance. Flash needs someone devoted, patient, and willing to put time into training and working through his reactivity toward other dogs. He truly is a special boy.

Another bleak day at the rescue with the rain pouring down non-stop, but it meant I got to spend some more quality time with the dogsI’ve never been one for poodles but Flash really has stolen my heartToday I really did see his loving and caring side as we cuddled away from the downpour. He rolled around, brought me his toys and chews before receiving belly rubs and head scratches until he fell asleep

If you’ve been to the rescue I’m usually walking Flash around the reception area in order for him to build his relationship with humans and other dogs but it was nice to just have some one on one time with him today, once you’ve created a bond with this boy he is yours for life.Flash’s’ training is still continuing and has managed to learn ‘touch’ where he boops his nose to the designated hand and is in the midst of learning ‘place’.

In a home will still need some training in order for him to thrive.

Flash is still a little mouthy when he gets super excited but is doing much better to when he first arrived with us.

It's been almost 4 months since Flash arrived here and I feel he has been very much overlooked. Deep down he’s a big cuddle bug and thrives on attention.

I tried spending most of my time with him today as kennel life is very stressful for him. We visited the big yard multiple times, went for a stroll and even met 2 dogs on a walk and greeted them with such kindness. Might I say he walks beautifully alongside you!

We met some new people in the reception area, which can sometimes make Flash a little uneasy but he did fantastically and even accepted some treats. For this he is muzzle trained as he can be mouthy when he’s a little too excited!

The last task of the day was to be groomed. I never thought I would see this boy sit still for more than a few seconds, but he amazed me and hopped up onto the grooming table ready to be pampered! And he now looks so good! Flash really doesn’t mind being groomed which will be helpful for the future.

I absolutely adore this beautiful boy. Flash is best described as an oversized puppy at heart. He has a playful, goofy nature but sometimes forgets his size, which can make him a little overexcited and mouthy, especially if he’s unsure or feeling frustrated.

Flash can be a sensitive soul who is often scared of his own shadow and needs plenty of reassurance and gentle confidence-building when faced with new experiences (like the umbrella he discovered today!). Because of this, kennel life is very tough for him, and I truly believe he will thrive so much more in a loving home.

Once Flash trusts you, he shows his affectionate side. He adores snuggling in for a cuddle and loves being pampered.

Flash’s ideal home would be an adult-only household where he can be the only dog. He isn’t aggressive with other dogs but can become boisterous and overwhelming. A quiet location with a secure, good-sized garden would be perfect, ideally with adopters who enjoy long walks and hikes.

He walks beautifully on the lead, enjoys all weathers, and has the stamina to go for hours. While he may sometimes react to new situations or get excitable around other dogs, his confidence and manners are improving every day. Flash will need adopters who are home most of the time or able to include him in their daily lives, as he finds being left alone difficult and may struggle with separation anxiety.

Flash is a truly striking Standard Poodle who has come to us from a home as his owners no longer had time for him and couldn't offer him the exercise he requires. Although he has lived in a home before, Flash is a worried boy who finds new experiences and people quite daunting. Because of this, Flash is looking for an experienced, adult only home with adopters who have plenty of time, patience, and love to give. He needs adopters who won’t give up on him and who will allow him to settle in at his own pace.Initially, Flash can be wary of new people and unsure of their intentions, but once you’ve gained his trust, he begins to show his affectionate and loyal nature. He forms strong bonds with those he feels safe around and shows a deep need for companionship. He does not cope well with being left alone and has shown signs of separation anxiety, getting very worried and anxious when left on his own. He will need an adopter who is able to be with him all of the time and who will work on this with him, slowly building up any time he is left. We are told he was crate trained in his previous home, which may help with this process.Flash is particularly sensitive to loud or sudden noises, which can cause him to panic. He feels safest in quiet, familiar surroundings and finds walks outside of the rescue grounds very difficult at the moment. When taken beyond his comfort zone, he becomes overwhelmed and spins in circles in an attempt to return to safety. While he can walk on a lead, his adopters will need to help him grow in confidence very gradually and may need to begin with simply enjoying time in the garden or short walks in peaceful areas.Flash would be best suited to being homed as an only dog as he can be grumbly when meeting other dogs. This is something his adopters will need to work on with him with patience and positive reinforcement. Because of this, he must only be allowed off lead in secure areas with no other dogs present. He also cannot live with cats or small animals and we are told he isn't a fan of pigeons or seagulls.Flash will be looking for a calm and quiet home, where he can settle in at his own pace and where his adopters will help him settle into a consistent routine so he can feel safe and secure. We are told that he is house trained but he may need some reminders of this in his new homeAlthough he is currently worried about going for walks, he is an active breed who will need to be kept both mentally and physically stimulated. We have been told that he has previously done some agility and scent work, so once his confidence grows this is something he may like to explore again in the future. His previous owners also said he enjoyed being groomed and bathed, though we haven’t yet introduced this at the rescue.This beautiful boy has so much potential in the right hands. If you have the time, experience, and understanding to give Flash the security and stability he needs, he will reward you with trust, loyalty, and love. Please don’t overlook him, he’s waiting for that one special person to believe in him.Flash has an entropion in both eyes, which has been repaired by our vets but does have some dermatitis around his eyes.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

name: Lassie
description:
I need an adult-only home where I can feel safe and loved.

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I cannot live with cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I can walk well on a lead and would love an active home.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have been spayed and am ready for my forever home.

Lassie has come to us from a breeder to find her forever home. When she first arrived she was a very nervous girl as she had not had much experience with people, so everything felt very new and overwhelming to her. She could be quite skittish when approached and was worried about meeting new people and being handled. Since then her confidence has started to really grow. Once she gets to know you, she is the most loyal, loving and affectionate girl and it's so wonderful to see.

Lassie will be looking for a calm and quiet, adult only home where she can settle in at her own pace. She would benefit from adopters who have breed experience and experience with worried dogs.

She will be looking for a home where there was at least one other kind and playful resident dog to be her friend and guide. She gets on brilliantly with all dogs here out and about and socialises well with a variety of breeds and sizes. However, we have found that she is most comfortable sharing her home with other Collies and will therefore need a home with at least one resident Collie. She cannot be homed with cats or small furries. She doesn't like to share her food with other dogs, so her adopters would need to ensure she was fed separately and would need to separate when feeding any long last treats or chews too.

Lassie now walks well on a lead and would love to find an active home where she can go on lots of walks and adventures with her new family and where her clever collie brain can be kept both mentally and physically stimulated. She is only used to walking in quiet areas, so would need gentle introduction to any busier places. Lassie has never lived in a home before so her adopters will need to be kind and understanding as she settles into the home and learns all about home life and new skills like house training.

With time, patience, and lots of love, Lassie will blossom. Once she has settled and gained confidence, she will become a cracking addition to her new family and will enjoy all the adventures life has to offer.

Lassie will need more than one meet and greet with her prospective adopters to allow her time to build a bond and feel comfortable with them before being adopted.

Lassie spent some time in the office today and coped really well. She happily tucked into a chicken foot for a tasty treat, although she couldn't resist pinching her kennel friend's one too! She was very curious about everything going on around her and enjoyed exploring her surroundings.

A member of staff she hadn't met before came into the office, and although Lassie was a little wary at first, she quickly settled and went back to happily enjoying her chicken foot. It was lovely to see her take the new experience in her stride and continue growing in confidence.

Beautiful beautiful Lassie! What a collie she is, she is so bright and has really come on so much with her basic training. Now knowing "sit" and "lie down". When we're out walking and a car is approaching, all I have to do is ask her to the verge and she sits and waits beautifully until we're able to start walking again. She really doesn't seem to be fazed by much traffic, but is very fixated with sheep if she comes across any. She passes horses lovely and will happily walk past other dogs no issue, but isn't keen on them approaching her. She's a big fan of food which helps with her training.She just needs people that understand that the girl she is when they first meet her is very worried but once she trusts you, her true personality really shines through.

Lassie is such a pretty girl who would do anything for you when she trusts you but is still wary of people initially. She did take treats off of a new person yesterday which was amazing to see. She needs to be in a collie experienced home with someone who will allow her the time to trust them at her own pace. She does love having a playfriend and she seems to be happiest when amongst other collies and collie crosses. She doesn't like sharing her food but is happy to share toys, beds and treats.

Lassie absolutely loves going out for her walks and gets very excited when she knows it’s time to head out. She really enjoys having a good sniff along the way and is always very interested in watching what’s going on in the fields as she passes by. Once she gets to know you, she is incredibly loving and affectionate, although she can take a little bit of time to warm up to new people. Lassie would still need a home with a resident dog, and she would truly thrive in a collie-experienced home where her needs and personality can be well understood.

Lassie is such a beautiful girl and once she knows you, she is the most affectionate, loyal and loving companion. She now walks well on a lead and absolutely loves her walks.

She will need a home with at least one other kind and confident resident dog who is a similar size to her and who loves to have a good play sometimes.

She doesn't like to share her food bowl, so he will need to be fed separately and this is something that her adopters will need to manage. In the right home she'll be someone's absolute best friend.

Lassie is a worried little lady who has come to us from a breeder. She has not had much experience with people, so everything feels very new and overwhelming to her. She can be quite skittish when approached, so she will need a calm, experienced home where she can be given the time and patience she needs to learn to trust.

At the moment, Lassie is scared of being touched, but she has already started to build a bond with her kennel mum and now allows gentle strokes from her. This shows that with consistency and kindness, shecanlearn to trust, and once she feels safe, she will begin to show her sweet side.

Lassie does walk on a lead, though she finds new experiences worrying, so she will need gentle introductions to the big wide world. She will also need to learn about house training and all the basics of living in a home, as these are things she has never known before.

Because Lassie is such a sensitive soul, she will need an adult-only home with no visiting children, and adopters who are happy to take things slowly at her pace. She will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home who can help her feel secure and show her the ropes. She gets on brilliantly with all dogs here and socialises well with a variety of breeds and sizes. She cannot be homed with cats or small furries.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Emrys
description:
I am looking for an adult only home with experienced adopters.

I could live with other dogs or be the only dog. I cannot be homed with cats.

I’m active and would love a large, secure garden to let off steam.

I have lots of energy and need an active home that loves adventures.

I am in a foster home now so am learning all about home life and house training.

Emrys is such a wonderful boy. He’s currently living in a foster home where has been for a year now and has absolutely flourished with them. He originally came to us from a farm so had very little in the way of training and socialising. This meant he could find new people worrying and during a short adoption of just a few months, he struggled to get used to a male visitor and would react towards him and redirected onto another person while stressed. He was returned to his foster home following this and hasn’t put a paw out of place since.

In foster, Emrys does very well, he absolutely adores his three collie best friends and we think he would thrive in a home with a resident dog to keep him company. We would consider an experienced home adopting him as an only dog however he loves having doggy friends so would need regular playdates. Unfortunately Emrys cannot live with cats. He is looking for a collie and rescue experienced home to help him thrive. He absolutely loves his walks and as he is such a clever lad, he will need brain games to wear out his mind too! If an active home is looking for a dog to join in on a sport like agility, we are sure he would do well!

Once Emrys trusts you, he is a wonderful companion and after an exciting walk where he gets to play with his friends, he does really enjoy a cuddle and a snooze on the couch. He is looking for a quiet, adult only home who understands collie behaviour, especially collies bred to work. When introduced to people with plenty of space to move away if he gets worried, he makes friends quickly. His amazing foster home have done so well in bringing out his silly, confident side and absolutely love his quirks. He is such a brilliant boy and has been waiting very patiently for his forever family.

Emrys has a fatty lump on the right flank that does not pose as an issue and is being monitored.

Emrys has been in foster with us for the past 10 months and has proven to be a confident, affectionate collie. He loves his walks just as much as he enjoys relaxing on the sofa afterwards.

He gets along well with both dogs and people, is clean in the house, and travels happily in the car. During his time with us, Emrys has shown no signs of reactivity and has greeted all visitors kindly.

He is a truly lovely dog and would make a fantastic, loyal companion.

Emrys has been in foster with us for seven months now, and he’s a real character. He gets on well with all dogs and, since being back with us, has shown no reactive or guarding behaviours. In the right home, he will truly thrive and make a wonderful companion.

Emrys can occasionally feel nervous around new people, and when this happens he takes himself off to his safe space, which is in the garden. From there, he’ll assess the situation at his own pace before coming back inside when he’s ready to meet someone new. It’s important that he’s given the time and space to do this calmly.

Once he knows you, Emrys is an absolute joy to be around. He loves his walks and secure field runs, is calm and settled in the evenings, and is incredibly affectionate.

With his wicked personality and loving nature, Emrys will make a fantastic companion for the right person.

Emrys has been back in foster for 11 weeks now and has settled in beautifully with no problems at all. He gets on very well with the three resident dogs as well as visiting dogs, and he is friendly with visitors and strangers outside the home. Emrys is totally house clean, travels well in the car, and is very treat-orientated, which makes him easy to train. After his walks, he loves nothing more than relaxing with his foster family.

Emrys has settled back in beautifully with his foster family and hasn’t shown any issues at all. He loves racing around the garden with the resident dogs and is starting to enjoy playing with toys and bringing a ball back.

He’s now completely house trained, walks very nicely on the lead, and is happy to travel in the car.

Emrys is such a lovable boy and is coming along wonderfully. He’ll make a fantastic companion for the right home.

Emrys is a striking, high-energy Collie who came to us from a farm where his owner could no longer give him the time he needed. After arriving he quickly found himself in foster, where he received plenty of exercise, mental stimulation, and affection, everything a clever Collie thrives on.He was adopted for a short time, but sadly returned to us after displaying behaviours in the home that hadn’t been seen while in foster. His adopters told us that Emrys exhibited some guarding tendencies and could react negatively when he felt backed into a corner or overwhelmed. Because of this, he’s looking for a calm, adult-only home with experienced adopters who understand the needs of a sensitive, intelligent dog and can support him with firm but kind guidance.Emrys will need a secure garden with space to run, and a home that can provide him with plenty of structured physical and mental activity to keep him happy and settled. He’s a bright boy who’s eager to please and loves learning. With patience, consistency, and the right support, Emrys has every potential to thrive.Emrys is now back with his original foster where he has shown many lovely qualities, he is clean in the house, travels well in the car, walks nicely on the lead, and is affectionate with those he trusts. He’s also food-motivated and already learning basic commands. We believe that Emrys thrives on activity and as he has not displayed these behaviours in foster it may be because he is provided with so much stimulation and exercises. His adopters will need to keep this in mind, and ideally be very active and willing to provide him with different games and activities to keep his mind busy.Emrys could live with another confident resident dog or be the only dog in the right home. He cannot live with cats, and due to his sensitivity, he will need a home without children or visiting children.This boy has so much potential and is just waiting for the right people to help him blossom.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Snowy
description:
I would like a home filled with love and kindness!

I need a laid back dog who is slightly larger than me in my new home.

I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home.

I am learning to walk on a lead and harness, but it's still very new for me.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have had 1 tooth extracted and am being treated for a ear infection.

Snowy is a very sweet little lady who has come to us from a breeder to find her forever home. When she first arrived she was quite worried and overwhelmed, but was trying so hard to be brave! She is still feeling quite timid, but once she gets to know she really enjoys having a fuss and is happy to be handled. She is an inquisitive and loving girl and once she's in a home we don’t think it will take long to come out of her shell and show her true personality.

Snowy would love to find a calm and quiet home with adopters who have experience with ex-breeding dogs and where she can settle in at her own pace and realise what it means to be a loved family member. We would consider homing her with gentle dog savvy older teenagers.

She takes great comfort from the company of other dogs, so she will need at least one kind, calm and confident resident dog in her new home to help her feel safe and settled. We have found that she is best suited to living with laid back dogs who are slightly larger than her. She can become a little jealous when other dogs are receiving attention and is more laid back with dogs who are a little bigger than her.

As Snowy has never lived in a home before, her adopters will need to be patient and understanding while she learns about home life and house training. She has started to learn all about walking on a lead and harness and while this is still very for her, she is doing so well.

With her sweet nature and growing confidence, we are sure it won’t take her long to settle into family life and flourish in a loving home.

Snowy has had 1 tooth removed at the time of her spay and has prevously been treated for an ear infection.

Snowy has been given the Gift of Life by Raychel Mount

This week Snowy has met a few new friends and has been out for her first walk! She took it all in her stride. We were very impressed with how well she has done.

She is a timid lady but really enjoys having a fuss when she gets to know you. She would love a home where she has at least one confident resident dog to help her to settle in. She is currently sharing her kennel with a laid back Scottie dog and gets along well with her, although she ideally needs a friend who is larger than her, she can be quite shy if they are too much larger than her.

She really is a sweetheart who is just looking for her chance at a new and happy life.

Snowy is a wonderful lady who has come to us from a breeder. She is happy to be handled and will shyly approach for fuss and attention, showing that she is trying so hard to be brave and really wants to make friends. Snowy is an inquisitive and loving girl and once she's in a home we don’t think it will take long to come out of her shell and show her true personality.Snowy would love to find and calm and quiet home with adopters who have experience with ex-breeding dogs and where she can settle in at her own pace and realise what it means to be a loved family member. We would consider homing her with gentle dog savvy older teenagers.She takes great comfort from the company of other dogs, so she will need at least one kind, calm and confident resident dog in her new home to help her feel safe and settled. We have found that she is best suited to living with laid back dogs who are slightly larger than her. She can become a little jealous when other dogs are receiving attention and is more laid back with dogs who are a little bigger than her.As Snowy has never lived in a home before, her adopters will need to be patient and understanding while she learns about house training and walking on a lead. With her sweet nature and growing confidence, we are sure it won’t take her long to settle into family life and flourish in a loving home.Snowy has had 1 tooth removed at the time of her spay and is being treated for infected ears.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Max
description:
I need an active, breed experienced home that loves playtime!

I’m active and would love a large, secure garden to let off steam.

I walk on the lead but I can pull when excited.

I am house trained but I need to learn some manners.

I will need to be neutered when I am of age! It's important!

Max is a superstar Lurcher who has been returned to us from his adoptive family. They made the heartbreaking decision to bring him back as he was becoming dominant over the other dog in the home.

He is a very excitable, happy boy - essentially an overgrown puppy in a big body. Max is full of fun and enthusiasm and will need an active home that can meet his exercise and stimulation needs. He already knows some basic commands such as “sit”, “paw”, “lay down”, and “wait”, and he is eager to interact with his people. He is very food oriented, which makes training enjoyable and rewarding, and he already shows lovely food manners, waiting patiently when asked.

Max absolutely loves other dogs and gets incredibly excited when he sees a potential new friend. However, he is still young, full of energy, and not always aware of his size and strength. This can sometimes lead to him becoming overexcited, a little mouthy, and unintentionally overwhelming other dogs. He has been working hard on his social skills and has been socialising while wearing a muzzle for safe and positive interactions. This is purely as a precaution, and his future adopters will need to continue this training and take a responsible approach when introducing him to other dogs.

As he was too much for a more timid dog in his previous home, we feel that Max would be best suited to being homed as an only dog, provided he has plenty of appropriate socialisation with dogs of a similar size and play style. He cannot live with cats or small furries.

As Max has previously lived in a home, he is familiar with household sights and sounds and is house trained. However, he is still working on his lead manners and can pull when he gets excited. Ongoing training, structure, and consistency will be important, along with plenty of mental stimulation to keep his busy mind engaged. He would thrive in a home that can offer activities such as training games or even dog sports.

Max could share his home with older, dog-savvy teenagers aged 16 and over, who understand that he can be a bit of a bull in a china shop. Any young people in the home will need to be confident and not worried by an enthusiastic dog who may jump up while he continues to work on his manners.

Max does struggle with separation anxiety and can become destructive if left alone for long periods, so he will need a home where someone is around most of the time and who is committed to helping him gradually build confidence with being left.

Max's new home will need a large, secure garden with 6ft boundaries, where he can let off some steam.

Max will make an amazing addition to a home with experience of similar breeds. He truly is a lovely boy, bouncy, affectionate, and full of life, with so much potential. He is a big-hearted lad who gives his whole heart, and with the right guidance, he will grow into an incredible companion.

Myself and Max have formed a very close bond over the last few months we’ve been working together. We take daily walks, go to the big play yard and have plenty of cuddles and fusses.I’m not sure what it is about Max that keeps getting him overlooked, it could be his size or that he is like a big playful puppy or something else, whatever it is, anyone would be lucky to have this handsome chap in their lives, he will always put a smile on your face and he has such a good sense of humour, watching him having zoomies in his kennel yard while he’s jumping off the obstacles in there then stoping then going back round again is nothing short of entertaining, or when he’s fishing treats out of the paddling pool while simultaneously blowing bubbles with his nose.Max truly deserves the world, he is such a sweet and caring boy with a big personality and so much love to give, as well as his big size there is also a big heart and all he wants is to be able to fully give it to someone and finally find his forever home.

Max is a big, happy baby with bags of energy and so much love to give. He is an excitable, affectionate boy who adores attention and loves having people around him. He is always happy to greet you and be part of whatever is going on.

As Max is still young and growing, he is slowly learning his manners and how to greet people politely without jumping. . He will need adopters who can match his energy, continue his training, and give him the stimulation, guidance, and love he needs to thrive.

Max is a gentle, overlooked soul who is learning new things every day at the rescue, but he is missing the comforts of home. He cannot wait to find a family who understands him and can help him blossom into the wonderful companion he is meant to be.With the right home, patience, and plenty of love, Max will make a loyal and joyful best friend.

Since coming back into our care, Max has been working hard on learning how to socialise with other dogs. He absolutely adores them and gets incredibly excited when he sees a potential new friend! Because he’s still young, full of beans, and a bit unaware of his own size, he can sometimes be a little too enthusiastic. This can lead to him becoming overexcited, a bit mouthy, and unintentionally overwhelming other dogs.

To help him learn safe and positive interactions, Max has been socialising while wearing a muzzle. This is purely for his safety and that of other dogs, and his future adopters will need to continue this training and take a responsible approach when introducing him to new canine companions. Aside from that, Max is everything you’d want in a young pup he's bouncy, happy, affectionate, and full of life.

He’s very food oriented, which makes training a dream! He already knows commands like “sit” and has learned great food manners where be waits patiently until he’s told he can eat. Max thrives on mental stimulation and ongoing training, and with the right guidance, he has all the potential to grow into an incredible dog. Max does struggle with separation anxiety and can become destructive if left alone for long periods. He really craves human company so he’ll need a home where someone is around most of the time and is committed to helping him build confidence being alone gradually. He’s such a loving, handsome boy who gives his whole heart, and it would be wonderful to see him back in a home environment where he can truly thrive. As a young pup, he’s missed out on some important early experiences, so a patient, committed family will make all the difference in helping him become the best version of himself.

He is a very excitable, happy boy - essentially an overgrown puppy in a big body. Max is full of fun and enthusiasm and will need an active home that can meet his exercise and stimulation needs. He already knows some basic commands such as “sit”, “paw”, “lay down”, and “wait”, and he is eager to interact with his people.

Max loves other dogs but is often unaware of his size and strength, having grown into such a big lad very quickly. As he is still just a puppy, he is still learning about manners and can be mouthy and plays in a very typical Lurcher way. In his previous home, there was an incident when he was around 3 months old, where he was a little too rough and resulted in a small dog needing veterinary treatment. He has started muzzle training for off-lead play as a precaution, and he will need a home that understands Lurcher play styles and is confident managing this appropriately. We have been socialising him with dogs of all sizes on walks and he is doing very well.

As Max has previously lived in a home, he is familiar with household sights and sounds and is house trained. However, he is still working on his lead manners and can pull when he gets excited. Ongoing training and consistency will be important.

Max will make an amazing addition to a home with experience of similar breeds. He truly is a lovely boy. He is a big-hearted, affectionate lad who is still convinced he can curl up and fit on your lap.

name: Lollipop
description:
I could live with older, dog-savvy teenagers who respect my space.

I need a doggy friend in my new home. I haven’t met cats so I’m unsure.

I haven't tried walks yet, a secure garden will be fine for now.

Please be patient with me, I need to learn house training and lead walking.

I have a grade 2 luxating patella and have had 7 teeth extracted. Read below for more

02-06-26 Lollipop is a lovely Bichon Frise who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. She is an inquisitive and funny little girl who is already showing glimpses of her playful personality. Lollipop will happily come over to lick our fingers before darting away again, as if she's still deciding whether people are quite as interesting as her kennel friends.

She definitely prefers the company of other dogs at the moment, but she is happy to follow their lead and takes great comfort from having them around. As Lollipop gets on wonderfully with her kennel friends, she will need a kind resident dog in her new home to help guide her and give her the reassurance she needs as she settles in and finds her paws.

As Lollipop has never lived in a home before, she will need to learn all about house training, lead training, and the other basics of home life. Her adopters will need to be patient and understanding as she adjusts to a whole new world and gains confidence in her surroundings.

Lollipop could share her home with older dog-savvy teenagers aged 16 and over who can appreciate and understand that she will need a gentle, quiet introduction to the home and her own space to settle in.

She has arrived with a Grade 2 luxating patella but her mobility appears unaffected and this may improve as she builds muscle tone. Lollipop has also arrived with severe periodontal disease and has had 7 teeth extracted.

Lollipop is an active and spritely little girl with a bright future ahead of her. With the support of a canine friend and a patient family, we hope her confidence will continue to grow and her charming personality will shine through even more.

Lollipop has been given the Gift of Life by Jodie Stanley

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Grape
description:
I need a calm and quiet home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I need a doggy friend in my new home. I haven’t met cats so I’m unsure.

I'd like a secure garden to potter around in.

I’ve never been on walks before and will need a gentle introduction.

Please be patient with me, I need to learn house training and lead walking.

Grape has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. She is a scared girl who is quite overwhelmed to find herself here at the moment and clearly isn't used to much human interaction, so finds this quite worrying at the moment. Despite her fears she will let you gently stroke her and is trying so hard to be brave.

She will be looking for a calm and quiet home where she can settle in at her own pace and would benefit from adopters with experience of ex-breeding dogs. She gets on wonderfully with her kennel friends and relies on their company for comfort, so she will need a kind resident dog in her new home to help guide her and give her the reassurance she needs as she settles in and finds her paws.

As Grape has never lived in a home before, she will need to learn all about house training, lead training, and the other basics of home life. Everything will be new to her, so a calm and consistent routine will be important.

Grape has arrived a little overweight, but with a good diet and gentle exercise, she should soon start to shed the excess. She has some hormonal alopecia, but this should start to grow back now she has been spayed. She has had 2 teeth extracted as well as a hernia repair and our vets have noted she has a small cyst, which was removed at the time of her spay. She is also being treated for a ear infection.

Grape is a very worried little girl at the moment, but with time, patience, and the support of another dog, she has the potential to slowly gain confidence and begin to show more of her true character.

Grape has been given the Gift of Life Wendy Stewart

Grape is still a nervous girlie and is taking longer to trust humans, given we know nothing of her past this is understandable.

I am trying to show her that all I want to do is help her and show her love, while also respecting her space and going slowly with her, which is what her adopters may have to do depending on where’s she’s at.

She will allow a gentle fuss when she’s in her bed but will not willingly come up and ask for one yet. I think maybe in a calm home she will start to feel more comfortable, kennel life isn’t for everyone, and learn to trust humans.

She is a beautiful girl and I hope she starts to trust soon so we can see that amazing and sweet personality that I’m sure is hiding in her somewhere just bursting to come out.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Crumble
description:
I need an adult-only, experienced home where I can feel safe and loved.

I want to be the only dog in the house. No cats please!

I’m still shy, so I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home.

I love my walks so one or two a day would be great!

I’ve never lived in a home, so I need to learn housetraining and lead walking.

I have Grade 2 luxating patella, which may improve with exercise.

Crumble is a lovely lady who came into our care from a breeder, searching for a fresh start. When she first arrived, she was very worried and unsure of the world around her, but with time and patience she has begun to settle beautifully. Crumble is now much less fearful of us humans and has really started to come out of her shell, choosing to engage more with staff members and showing curiosity about her surroundings. She seems to be gaining confidence each day, which is wonderful to see.

While she can still be sensitive and appreciates having her space respected, it’s clear that Crumble simply needed time to feel safe. With gentle reassurance and plenty of TLC, her true personality is beginning to shine through. She is a gentle soul who responds best to calm, kind interactions and slow introductions.

She would be happiest and thrive best as the only dog in the home, where se can enjoy all the attention, settle more easily into a routine, and build a strong bond with her new family.

As she has never lived in a home environment before, she will need patient support with house training and learning everyday routines. She will need a home without cats as she is overly interested.

We are looking for an experienced, adult-only home where Crumble can continue to build her confidence at her own pace. A quiet household with understanding adopters who are happy to let her take things slowly will allow her to truly flourish. With the right people, time, and lots of love, Crumble will make a wonderfully rewarding companion.

Crumble has a previous bite wound inside her ear, which is currently being treated and our vets have noted that she has a Grade 2 luxating patella. This doesn't cause her any issues and may improve with gentle exercise as she builds muscle.

There’s not a bad word you could say about this girly pop. She is goofy, affectionate, playful, friendly and gaining confidence by the day! She has been trying her best to be kennel clean, no encouragement needed.

She is a staff favourite and that girls little teefys are part of her personality! Nibbling at you to show you just how much she loves to play with you, entertains her pal Criss cross and her kennel mums in the yard.

She belongs in a home where her people can show her the best doggy life imaginable while also understanding that Crumble has also not known much of the world and requires a bit of patience and care when handling her.

Taking every opportunity to play and zoom around with her best friend, Criss Cross.Her confidence is growing and enjoying her walks and greeting new people at her own free will as long as she is guaranteed some fuss or treats.

She enjoys sprawling out on the floor with her chew toy just enjoying the vibes.When she finds her home she will be very eager in sharing her affectionate nature with her family

Crumble is becoming a very happy chow now and has recently discovered that there's no limit on zoomies. She does get along with her chow friend, Criss Cross, but isn't keen on other dogs, so is looking for a home as an only. She is wary of people initially, but once you've gained her trust she becomes your absolute best mate. She loves her walks and gets very excited when she sees you bringing her harness towards her. She's a big foodie too and loves treats.

Crumble is coming on so beautifully and it truly makes my heart melt. Today we had some cuddle and play time in her kennel, and it was such a special moment. For a girl who once wasn’t very happy with touch, she’s now rolling over for belly rubs, snuggling in her bed, and even playing “paw paw” with me.

She’s also learning about wearing a harness and is improving every day. If you have a Crumble shaped hole in your life, she really is something special.

Crumble is a worried lady who came into our care from a breeder, searching for a fresh start. When she first arrived, she was very worried and unsure of the world around her, but with time and patience she has begun to settle beautifully. Crumble is now much less fearful of us humans and has really started to come out of her shell, choosing to engage more with staff members and showing curiosity about her surroundings.

Crumble will need a confident and friendly resident dog who can help guide her and show her the ropes. As she has never lived in a home environment before, she will need patient support with house training, lead walking, and learning everyday routines. But will need to take things slow to start with some garden sniffs adventures on the lead may be too scary to start.

Crumble has a previous bite wound inside her ear, which is currently being treated.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

name: Criss Cross
description:
I need an adult-only experienced home that understands me.

I'd prefer to be the only dog in the home and no cats please!

I’m still shy, so I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home.

I can now walk on a lead and harness and enjoy my walks.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I am neutered and ready to find my forever home!

Meet Criss Cross, a big-hearted, sensitive lad who has come to us from a breeder and is slowly learning to find his confidence in the world. When Criss Cross arrived as a worried soul, needing extra patience, understanding, and gentle care. He had very little formal handling in his past, and early on could be grumbly or anxious when unsure. He required slow, careful introductions to people, places, and experiences while he started to understand his new surroundings.

While Criss Cross can still be a little shy when meeting new people, once he feels comfortable his affectionate side really shines through. He actively seeks out fuss and cuddles and has become quite the cuddle bug, often trying to return kisses to the people he trusts. He is learning his manners well and responds nicely to calm, consistent guidance.

Criss Cross is growing in confidence with his harness training and now enjoys daily walks beyond the rescue. He has even started greeting some people on his walks for a hello and perhaps a treat or a fuss. He can still feel a little unsure if people are behind him, but gentle encouragement and reassurance help him relax and continue exploring the world around him.

He has also started learning about kennel cleanliness, with staff helping him practice good toilet habits to make the transition into a home life easier for him in the future. When enjoying some quiet time on his own, Criss Cross loves exploring his toy collection, especially anything involving treats or food puzzles.

Criss Cross would prefer to be the only dog in the home so he can fully relax and enjoy all the attention for himself.

He will need a calm, adult-only home with experienced adopters who can continue helping him build confidence at his own pace. He also appreciates having a quiet space at mealtimes where he feels safe and secure. Criss Cross will need a home without cats as he is overly interested in them.

Criss Cross is a truly gorgeous, sweet-natured gentle giant with so much love to give. With patience, understanding, endless bum scratches and plenty of tasty treats, he has every chance to flourish into the loyal and devoted companion he is meant to be.

Criss Crosswill need more than one meet and greet with his prospective adopters to allow him time to build a bond and feel comfortable with them before being adopted.

Criss Cross is such a sweetheart! He is loyal to his friend Crumble, his kennel mums and dads. He’s still greeting everyone on walks with a bit of caution but once he’s recognised you, you’re considered family.

He’s going on occasional independent walks to gain his confidence but he is always looking for Crumble to keep him company. He’s very cautious of new things however day by day his confidence is growing and with his trust in his kennel mums he always runs back to check they’re okay, showing you just how brave he is and continues to be, so when the right home finds him….they will have to earn his trust and give him the patience and love he truly deserves.

This distinguished gent is progressing so well, every day!

He has started to get in the practice of being kennel clean, one of his kennel mums likes to come in a little early to help him practice this so when he finds his forever home it might be a little less stressful learning toilet training skills properly.

Criss Cross is even getting more adventurous greeting people on his daily walks now just to  say hello and maybe scoring a treat or fuss, and even recognising some of the staff when they visit him.

He is protective of Crumble and always looks to see her catching up on their daily walks, and enjoys playing with her or being near watching over her. But when he has his solo kennel time, he has been exploring his personal toy collection, anything with a treat in it and he’s happy. This cuddle bug deserves a home with endless bum scratches and scrumptious treats

This handsome guy is progressing every day! And showing his kennel mums just how much of a sweetheart he is. He is an affectionate goofball once he’s comfortable with you. He enjoys playing with his chow friend Crumble and he loves getting love from her and his kennel mums. He loves to jump up to have his harness put on, refusing to stand still because he gets so excited to go out and explore with Crumble.

Criss Cross will now even allow some of his part time kennel mums and dads to give him attention too. He is still very cautious of new people but is slowly meeting new people and remembering them!

With some time his new family will find out just how special Criss Cross is!

Criss Cross has made incredible progress over the last few days. He used to hide and grumble when people approached his kennel, but this has changed massively!

Now, Criss Cross comes up to the kennel door and wags his tail, getting very excited to see people. Spending time with this handsome boy brings me so much joy; I often find myself straying from my section just to sneak in five minutes of cuddles.

Criss Cross is still looking for a quiet, gentle home and someone who will take things slow while he gets to know them.

Criss-Cross has grown into an incredibly sociable and interactive boy, although he can still be a little shy when first meeting new people. Once he feels comfortable, he actively seeks out attention and loves a cuddle, often trying to give kisses in return. Criss-Cross is a big lad and is still learning his manners; when he’s excited he can occasionally use his size to try and barge past, but he responds well to clear, firm verbal guidance.

He will need a quiet area to enjoy his meals, as this helps him feel more secure. Criss-Cross is doing very well with his harness training and has already ventured out of the rescue for walks, managing a good distance down the road. He can still feel a little unsure if people are walking behind him, and one of his endearing little quirks is that he finds reassurance if you turn and face him while walking backwards. Criss-Cross is a truly gorgeous, sweet and gentle giant who will thrive in an active, patient and understanding home.

Criss Cross is a very worried soul who has come to us from a breeder, and is needing extra patience, understanding, and tender care. He appears to have had very little formal handling in his past and can be grumbly or anxious when he feels uncomfortable. He requires slow, careful introductions and time to begin to understand the world around him.

Criss Cross would thrive in an adult-only home with experienced owners who can provide the reassurance and consistency he needs to start building trust. He is still learning the basics of house training and walking on a lead, so his adopters will need to be patient and gentle as he gradually discovers confidence. He will need an understanding and kind resident dog in the home he can look to as he continues to learn and trust.

This sweet, sensitive boy is not yet ready for the full bustle of the world, but with time, quiet encouragement, and safe garden sniffing adventures, he will slowly open up and reveal the loyal companion he can be. Criss Cross will need a calm, loving home where his cautious nature is respected and nurtured and where he can finally feel safe and truly belong.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

name: Cloe
description:
I need an adult only home that understands ex-breeders.

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I have never met cats.

I need an extra secure garden and home that can accommodate my needs.

I have never been on walks, but may be too scared and will need some time.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have had 7 teeth extracted and an inguinal hernia repaired. Read below for more!

Cloe is a very worried little lady who has come to us from a breeding environment and is still learning that people can be kind. It is clear she has had very little positive experience with humans in the past, and as a result she finds trust incredibly difficult. She can be flighty when worried and may choose to protect herself if she feels pressured or uncomfortable.

Cloe is a sweet but complex girl who has already shown us that, once she truly trusts someone, she has a wonderful and affectionate side. With her favourite member of staff, the very first person who cared for her when she arrived, she is a completely different dog. She happily approaches for attention, enjoys gentle fusses, is comfortable being picked up, and greets him with excitement and affection. Watching the bond they have built is incredibly special and shows just how much potential Cloe has when given the time she needs to feel safe.

Since arriving with us, Cloe has continued to make lovely progress. There is now a real sparkle in her eyes that seems to grow brighter every day. She is much perkier, always happy to see familiar faces, and is even beginning to show a mischievous little side that makes everyone smile. She has become an absolute joy to care for and has settled into her daily routine beautifully.

Although trust does not come easily to Cloe, she is becoming increasingly comfortable with the people she knows. She is now much more accepting of being picked up and handled and takes these interactions in her stride. She recently enjoyed a pamper session in our grooming room, where she happily allowed her beautiful coat to be brushed and a few small matts to be clipped away, taking the whole experience in her stride. Moments like these are a wonderful reminder of just how far she has already come.

Cloe has also recently started her harness training. Although she is still a little too timid to venture out on walks just yet, she is making a fantastic start and we are confident that, with a little more time and encouragement, she will soon be ready to begin exploring the world beyond the rescue.

Cloe will need a calm and quiet adult-only home with no visiting children and adopters who have experience of very worried ex-breeding dogs. Her new family will need to understand that building a relationship with her is likely to take time and that progress will come through patience, consistency, and allowing her to make her own choices.

The best way to win Cloe over is to let her approach on her own terms. She needs people who can resist the temptation to rush her and instead allow her to work out for herself that they are safe and trustworthy. Once she reaches that point, we believe she has the potential to become a truly loving and devoted companion.

Cloe absolutely loves the company of other dogs and will need a kind, calm, and confident resident dog in her new home. Having a canine friend gives her comfort and reassurance and will help guide her as she learns about life beyond the rescue. We have also discovered that Cloe is not comfortable around cats and can become quite fixated by them, so she will need a cat-free home.

As she has never lived in a home before, Cloe's adopters will need to be patient as she learns all about home life, including house training and other everyday routines. She has now started to become familiar with wearing a harness, and while she is not yet ready to walk on lead, her adopters should continue this training gently and positively, allowing her confidence to grow at her own pace.

Although progress with Cloe may be slow at times, she continues to surprise us with just how much she is blossoming. She is becoming more affectionate with every passing week, and it has been a privilege to watch her begin to enjoy life. In a loving home where she has all the time in the world to build trust, alongside a kind resident dog, we believe she will flourish into an incredibly devoted companion. She has already waited far too long for the family she deserves, and we can't wait to see her finally find it.

Cloe has had 7 teeth extracted at the time of her spay, as well as an inguinal hernia repaired. She also had a lump removed from her right upper eyelid.

Cloe has been given the Gift of Life by Carol Unsworth

Progress with Cloe is still very slow, but it is there and it is happening, so that’s all that matters. Since my last update she has opened herself up to me ever so slightly, and now gets very excited to see me everything morning and will run up to me and sniff my fingers with a waggy tail. I know that may not sound like a lot to most, but for a dog like Cloe it’s a big step forward.

I mentioned in my last update that I had to take her into the vets a few times and it made her more frightened of me, so since then I’ve completely left her be as I felt as though we needed to reset. I’ve spent a lot of time with her, just sitting down and being present, but no touching (until today). She wasn’t happy about me picking her up and was trying to bite the blanket I was holding, but she put up way less of a fight than she did a few weeks ago … so I’m taking that as a win!! I took her outside of her kennel just to try and take some nice photos of her and she coped really well. When we were finished and I put her back in her kennel, I got down on the floor and wiggled my fingers at her to see if she was still my friend, and thankfully she did come over with a waggy tail, so that made me happy.

Over the next few weeks I’m going to continue some gentle handling like today, every so often so that she gets more used to it, but it also won’t overwhelm her. I’ll listen to her and what she’s telling me .. stop if it’s too much, and do a little more if she’s ready.I really love Cloe and I’m understanding her more and more each day, and I think she feels the same way about me. I’m starting to feel hopeful!!

Cloe is a sweet but complex little girl who has clearly been through a great deal in her life. She has major trust issues with humans and so far only trusts one person out of all the staff that work here. At the rescue we see 2 sides of her. One side is a happy, excitable girl who enjoys a little fuss and is fine to be picked up (by her favourite person), and the other side is a scared girl who would rather be left alone (by everyone else). Her favourite person here is the first person that worked with her when she first arrived, so I think she sees him as her ‘rescuer’ which is very sweet. She definitely trusts him and it’s quite shocking to see the difference in her when she’s around him - in a good way! With everyone else, she’s still very standoffish and doesn’t tolerate being touched or handled at all. She will nip to protect herself, but this doesn’t mean she’s a nasty dog at all .. she’s simply just that scared.I’ve been working with Cloe for about a month now and even though she’s a lot more comfortable around me now than she used to be, she definitely still doesn’t trust me. I think it’s because I had to take her into the vets a couple of times when she first came over to my section, and it’s definitely stunted our relationship. Normally with dogs like Cloe, I won’t handle them for a good while until I establish some form of bond with them, but I couldn’t do this with Cloe as she had to see the vet. Since then, I’ve been leaving her to her own devises and trying to win her over from afar. Although she still hardly approaches me, she’s more relaxed in my presence now .. so at least we are making progress.I think in Cloe’s case, the best way to work with her is to just leave her come to you first. If she can work out by herself that you’re kind and not a threat, you’re heading in the right direction!! And then when she trusts you, she’ll drop her guard with you and become a little sweetheart like she is with her favourite staff member here. In a home where she has all the time in the world to bond with her new parents, I think she will absolutely blossom!!! But it may take a little while to get there, so her future adopters will need to be very understanding and patient with her. It will be SO worth it though, I promise!

Cloe is a very worried little lady who has come to us from a breeding environment and is not yet comfortable with being handled. It’s clear she has had very little experience of kindness, so she can be quite flighty and may panic if approached too quickly and can choose to make the wrong decision if pushed out of her comfort zone.

Cloe settles quickly once she feels safe and has already begun making lovely progress. With her favourite member of staff she is already showing her funny and affectionate side and is happy to approach him, kiss his fingers and will even do a little excited dance. With patient, understanding adopters who can appreciate why she may feel scared or defensive and support her as she builds trust, we believe she will settle in and start to come out of her shell.

She will need a calm and quiet adult-only home with no visiting children and with adopters who have experience of scared ex-breeding dogs and who are willing to give her the patience, kindness, and time she needs to truly thrive.

Cloe is already starting to approach you slowly on her own terms and so will need plenty of time, gentle encouragement, and extra TLC to continue building her confidence. She absolutely loves the company of other dogs and will need a kind, calm, and confident resident dog in her new home to help guide her as she adjusts to home life.

As she has never lived in a home before, Cloe's adopters will need to be kind and understanding as she settles in and learns all about home life and new skills like house training. She doesn't yet walk on a lead and harness and will need to practice around the home and garden once she is more comfortable with being handled.

Cloe has had 7 teeth extracted at the time of her spay as well as an inguinal hernia repaired. She had a lump on her right upper eye lid that has been removed.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Toucan
description:
I would like an active, adult-only home with plenty of adventures and love!

I need to be the only dog in the home, and cannot live with cats!

I’m active and would love a large, secure garden with 6ft+ fences to play in.

I have lots of energy and need an active home that loves adventures.

I am in a foster home and am house trained.

I am neutered and ready to find my forever home!

Toucan is a very handsome and active boy who originally came to us from a breeder. He was adopted for a short period of time, but unfortunately found himself back in rescue.

Toucan is a bright and friendly boy who enjoys meeting new people and, given time to build a bond, forms lovely relationships with his humans. He is affectionate and engaging, and with the right guidance, will make a wonderful addition to a committed home.

He is an active lad who loves his walks and exploring the world around him. At present, he can be strong on the lead as he is eager to get out and sniff everything he sees, so his adopters will need to continue working on his lead training and help him learn to walk more calmly. He is currently being trained to walk on a halti here. His absolute favourite thing in the world is playing with a ball and he just lives to play fetch! He would choose his ball over a treat any day and has proven himself to be the best ball catcher the rescue has seen!

Toucan is a clever boy who is picking things up quickly. He will benefit from a consistent routine and ongoing training to help him feel secure and understand expectations. Providing him with structure, alongside quiet resting spaces where he won’t be disturbed, will be key in helping him manage any anxieties. Although he has lived in a home, he may need some reminders about home life and skills like house training as he settles back into home life.

Toucan was adopted into a family home and though he loved the whole family, there was one isolated incident where he was snoozing in his bed and was accidentally startled by the older child in the home. Waking suddenly and feeling frightened, Toucan reacted in panic and gave a nip before immediately moving away. We believe this response was purely due to being startled while asleep, but for this reason, Toucan will be looking for an adult-only home where there are no visiting children. His adopters will need to provide him with a quiet, safe space where he can rest undisturbed and feel completely relaxed.

Toucan spent some time in a foster home, but sadly it became clear that his preference was to be an only dog and he didn't enjoy sharing his home with another dog. He does get along well with other dogs when out and about and is fine passing them on walks etc, he would just prefer not to share his space with them. We think he is going to love being the only dog in the home where he can get all the love and attention for himself.

Our vets have noted that his back legs are slightly deflected outward, but this isn’t causing him any issues.

Toucan is a fun, intelligent, and affectionate boy who, with the right experienced adopters, routine, and understanding, will grow into a fantastic and rewarding companion.

Toucan has been given the Gift of Life by Alison Hale

Another month has gone by here at the rescue and still Toucan remains, poor boy isn’t having any luck.

Recently he’s started going out for long walks with one of our experienced volunteers and to say he loves it is an understatement, come rain or shine this boy loves to be outside, either walking or running around the big yard chasing his ball.

The lady that was fostering him also comes up regularly to spend time with him and he gets so excited when he sees her, which shows that once he loves you, he loves you forever and you have a friend for life.

Toucan is a very smart boy and you can’t really fool him by pretending to throw the ball because he catches on quickly that you haven’t, also he is very good at knowing where I’m pointing to. I don’t mean if I’m next to something and pointing right at it, I mean if he can’t find his ball for example, I can point in the direction it went and he’ll look at me and go that way, very very smart boy.

As much as I love Toucan and really enjoy his company, it’s time now for him to find his forever home, I know there’s someone out there for him, the right person could be reading this right now. If you think it could be you but your hesitant, don’t be, honestly Toucan is an amazing dog and everyone that has met him has nothing but lovely things to say about him.
