Toucan spent some time in a foster home, but sadly it became clear that his preference was to be an only dog and he didn't enjoy sharing his home with another dog, so he has come back to kennels to continue the search for his perfect home.

Toucan is an absolute angel, but he does find kennel life quite stressful and can become vocal when unsettled. As a result of kennel stress, he can lose weight, so I’m keeping a close eye on him and making sure he’s getting all the nutrition and care he needs.

Toucan is a very active boy! His favourite thing in the world? A ball! He lives to play fetch. I can offer him a treat, but if there’s a ball around he’s much more interested in that. He is the best ball catcher at the rescue, the focus he has on that tennis ball is honestly something I admire. Besides his love for all things round, he is a genuinely sweet boy, who enjoys a fuss and of course a walk. He can be strong on a lead so for this reason I’m currently halti training him and although he’s not a huge fan yet, he is doing really well with it and is currently tolerating it.

Toucan is very trainable and tries his best to figure out what you’re asking him. He’s very smart and it breaks my heart that this beautiful boy is spending his days in kennels instead of out there in the world, playing fetch to his hearts content.

If you’re looking for a smart, active and adventurous best friend then please consider Toucan, he really is an amazing dog and he’s very handsome too.

Toucan is a lovely boy who has settled in as though he’s always been part of the family. He’s very confident and affectionate, and has happily met family and friends - he loves everyone, including my 17-year-old nephew.

He has a big love of food (including everyone else’s!), so he is fed in his crate using a slow feeder bowl to help him pace himself. He needs to gain some weight, so we’ve been adding extra protein to his biscuits and he’s already starting to look healthier.

Toucan is house clean and travels brilliantly in the car, happily jumping in and out. He gets on amazingly well with the four dogs in the home as well as friends’ dogs.

He is comfortable with the crate and, although the door is now left open at night, he still chooses to sleep in there. Toucan can pull on the lead, which we’re currently working on. He isn’t bothered by loud noises and absolutely loves playing ball - he’s fantastic at fetch.

Toucan is a very handsome and active boy who originally came to us from a breeder. He was adopted for a short period of time, but has unfortunately found himself back in rescue. Toucan was adopted into a family home and though he loved the whole family, there was one isolated incident where he was snoozing in his bed and was accidentally startled by the older child in the home. Waking suddenly and feeling frightened, Toucan reacted in panic and gave a nip before immediately moving away. We believe this response was purely due to being startled while asleep, but for this reason, Toucan will be looking for an adult only home, where there are no visiting children. His adopters will need to provide him with a quiet, safe space where he can rest undisturbed and feel completely relaxed. Please don't let this put you off, as he is truly a wonderful boy and his previous adopters have said how wonderful he is and will be in the right home.

Toucan is is always on the go, eager to explore, and looking for his next task. He has a vibrant, happy-go-lucky personality and would thrive in an active home that can provide plenty of attention, as well as physical and mental stimulation. He is super smart and loves to use his brain and already knows "sit", "paw" and "down." He is a lovely boy who is very affectionate and really enjoys having cuddles and fuss once he gets to know you.

Toucan is confident enough that he could be homed as an only dog as long as his adopters had plenty of time to spend with him. He could also be homed with another kind resident dog and has shared his kennel well with dogs at the rescue. He can sometimes be reactive to other dogs when passing their kennels but is fine when out and about on lead. He still needs some training on the lead as he likes to be in charge, pulling us along on his travels.

Toucan has learned to walk on a harness, but he can pull and so will need adopters who can manage this and who are willing to work on this with him. He is obsessed with tennis balls and will play all day if you had the time, so he really hopes his new family will enjoy throwing balls for him!

Toucan can be a little greedy, so at the moment he is fed separately from any other dogs. He is a little and so he is now on a raw food diet to help him gain weight.

He had never lived in a home before his adoption and so home life is very new to him and he will need kindness and patience as he settles in. He was clean in his previous home but may have some accidents as he settles into a new home. With kindness, consistency, and plenty of opportunities to burn off his energy, he will make a wonderful, loyal companion for the right family.

Our vets have noted that his back legs are slightly deflected outward, but this isn't causing him any issues.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

name: Margate
description:
I need an experienced adult only home with no visiting children.

I would prefer to be an only dog, though may be able to live with a calm female.

I’m active and would love a large, secure garden to let off steam.

I have lots of energy and need an active home that loves adventures.

I can resource guard and will need adopters who can manage this.

I’m fully vaccinated and neutered. I have received treatment for an ear infection.

Margate has now spent over2 and a half years in kennels. That’sover 900 daysof waiting. Waiting while thousands of people have walked past his kennel. Waiting while his friends have come and gone. Waiting for someone to truly see him.

Everyone here adores Margate - he’s smart, affectionate, and bursting with personality. But love from staff isn’t the same as a forever home, and Margate is more than ready for his next chapter.

Since staying with us, Margate has received a dental treatment and had a cyst removed from his back left leg.

Margate came to us from a breeder in November 2023. He'sincredibly intelligent,loves a challenge, and will do absolutely anything for a tasty treat. He picks up tricks like lightning - he knows “sit”, “paw” and “speak” and he’s always ready to learn more. He thrives when his brain is engaged and would be an excellent candidate for scent work or activities like cani-cross (on-lead only!). He’s been described asone of the smartest dogs we’ve ever worked with.

He'svocal, a bit cheeky, and loves to tell us when it’s breakfast time - you can hear his excited barks all the way from the car park. He may come off as bouncy and bold, but beneath the surface, Margate is a sensitive soul who, sadly, has beenlet down by humans before. He can get worried easily, especially in overwhelming environments like the vet, where he becomes a bit of a puddle of anxiety (though he's muzzle-trained and manages with support).

Margate does require avery specific kind of home, and we won’t sugar-coat it - he’s not a “starter dog.” He needs:

Margate ispossessive around food, toys, and bedding, likely stemming from insecurity in his past. These behaviours are manageable - we’ll give guidance - but his adopters will need tounderstand and work with him, never punish or push him beyond his limits. Setting him up for success is the key.

Margate will test you - not because he's difficult, but because he's clever, spirited, and sensitive. But once you have his trust, you’ll havea bond that’s unbreakable.

Because Margate isn’t a dog you can bring home on a whim. He’s forsomeone extraordinary. Someone who sees past the behaviours and looks into his big, soulful eyes and says,“I’ve got you now.”

Margate has come so far during his time with us - he’s learned manners, made friends, gained confidence, and shown us theincredible potentialhe holds. But he deserves so much more than life in a kennel, no matter how much we love him.

Margate isnot for everyone, but if he’s for you - truly for you - then you are in for the mostrewarding journey. He’ll love you with his whole heart, and you’ll wonder how you ever lived without him.

If you think you could behis person, please don’t wait another day.

Margate’s forever is long overdue. Let’s not make him wait another year.

Margate will need more than one meet and greet with his prospective adopters to allow him time to build a bond and feel comfortable with them before being adopted.

“My boy, Margate… I see all of you, and I’ll love you like you’re my own until your forever person comes along. I’ll be so jealous of whoever gets to share their life with you.”

Mr Margate has utterly blown me away in recent times.Around a month ago, Margate had an unfortunate accident in the play yard, which lead to an emergency vet visit and his front paw needing stitches.

As one of the kennel managers, I see and interact with Margate regularly, but unfortunately I don’t get the luxury of working closely with him. But I think the story below highlights how much this beautiful boy wants to do the right thing.

I was the first person to get to Margate after his accident, do you know what he did when I got to the yard? He limped over to me, sat at my feet and cried out. I know some will say it’s anthropomorphism, but I am so sure in that moment, he was asking for help!

We had to carry Margate up to the top of the rescue to clean him off and assess his wounds, Margate is muzzle trained which meant we could safely assess his wounds without any risk - but honestly, he was SO well behaved. Yes he needed reassurance that he was, in-fact, being a good boy, but he blew me away with the trust he gave us.

We cleaned him up and quickly realised he needed to go for assessment with an out-of-hours vet. So, phone calls were made, and off he went, carried into a strange car and taken to a strange place and sedated for stitches… where again, he was such a good boy despite the pain he must have felt.

Upon return, poor Margate was on strict rest, with only short toilet walks, having bandage changes every few days, boots on and off for walks… his whole routine was turned upside down. Living in kennels, his world is already quite small, and these changes, although temporary and for his own good, made his world even smaller.

This is where Margate began to struggle a little; in pain and insecure, some of his guarding behaviours (which we haven’t seen for a long time) did resurface. Luckily, Margates main carer knows him inside & out, like the back of her hand, and created a plan for everyone to follow to help build his confidence back up. Just some small adjustments to keep him & everyone around him safe, which worked beautifully and we got to see the best of our boy again, despite still being on rest.

A few weeks have now passed, Margate has had his stitches out, his wound has healed beautifully - and he fully back to being our incredible, charismatic, clever, fun loving boy! It’s the highlight of my day to see Margate, he is so happy meeting everyone.

All of this is to say, Margate may not always be an “easy” dog, but he SO wants to do the right thing. Occasionally, his worries overwhelm him, but for someone with experience and patience, the love and joy you will receive in return will be so worth it.

Margate is, hand on heart, one of the most phenomenal dogs I’ve ever met, and after nearly nine years at Many Tears, I’ve met a lot of dogs! He is going to be someone’s absolute best friend and the most wonderful adventure companion.

His profile already covers all of his basic needs, so I won’t repeat those here, but I can honestly say that not a single day he’s spent in kennels has been wasted.

He has grown, learned, and loved so much during his time with us. His confidence has blossomed, he absolutely adores people, and he has made some lovely doggy playmates. He knows exactly which treats are his favourites, and he’s settled beautifully into his routine. He heads out to his yard at 9am, then off to reception to spend time with more people (and, of course, collect more treats!).

Throughout the day, different staff members make time to visit him for extra cuddles and attention, and he often spends my entire lunch break relaxing in the yard on sunny days. This is something he enjoys every second of.

Margate is such a genuinely lovely guy, which makes it all the more surprising that he’s had to wait so long. He has the sweetest nature and seems to want nothing more than to be everyone’s best friend. While he’s here, he’s made some great female play friends and absolutely loves spending time with them every day, soaking up the companionship and fun.

In one month's time Margate will have been waiting a full two years for his forever family to find him. He's been in our care for a full 23 months, that's around 700 days, he's never been adopted or gone to foster so he's not been returned to us, he just has been in the rescue for all that time, waiting.

The first picture is his original booking in photo, a very worried boy who arrived knowing nothing about what we would do for him and how much love he'd come to find in this place that should be a pit stop until their forever home is found. But he slowly grew to love people, he started to let his true personality shine through and he started to learn how to be the goodest boy in so many ways. We flash forward to today and I can still see his insecurities at times, these may never truly go away, but we help him through it all and he is one of the best dogs I have ever known.

I love him as I love my own adopted dogs and I try to let him have as much time and love as I can give him to help him not feel like a single day in kennels is wasted. Because this is two years of his life that he won't get back, so I damn well hope that I, and everyone else who has had a part in his journey, makes every single moment count!I know he'll always have his quirks and he does need a specific type of home with people who understand and can positively manage his guarding behaviours so that he doesn't feel the need to show these often. But he's such a good boy, he's so loved by all of us at the rescue, he's so smart and beautiful and funny and loving and such good company. I love every speckle of white he's grown on his face in these two years, I love every snuggle we have and every single moment he looks to me to let me know that everything is going to be okay.He needs the right home but more importantly, he deserves the right home, the right people. If I could move heaven and earth to give him his perfect life, I would.I do want him to leave the rescue, but only to people that can fully commit to keeping him safe and listening to every single guideline and boundary that we give them. One of which is that HE MUST be kept on a lead at all times when out of fully secure areas, his prey drive is very high and he deserves to be given everything he needs to be kept safe from being harmed, which means keeping him on a lead!I shall continue to love him with every day that he's in my care, as well as every day after he finds his perfect home and I wave him off.I'll be so happy that he was chosen but he's going to leave a huge hole in my heart. He's truly one in a billion.If you feel you may be the right fit, please read his profile completely and fill in a very detailed application form. We will be very particular about where he goes, because that is the least he deserves from us.My handsomest boy, Mr Margate

It's strange really, the bond you have with a dog on your section, especially if they're a long stayer.

They aren't your dog and yet you love them as though they are, you cuddle them and tell them how amazing they are, you pour your heart and soul in to them and try your very hardest to make each and every day special in some way because their time in kennels shouldn't be a waste or simply a means to an end.. it should matter and they should be living their life whilst waiting for a family of their own.

This is hard to do when there are so many dogs to care for, but you try to do the best for them and to make sure they know how truly special they are.

This is very much the case for my best friend, Margate (AKA Maggie, Margaret, Marjorie, Marge and he responds to all these names). He is the epitome of special, he does need a specific home with people who don't put him in bad situations, but when the right people come along, they'll be adopting a dog that will give them an absolutely smashing best mate and although I am desperate for his forever home to find him, the thought of saying goodbye to the most special boy with a heart the size of the moon and a brain that's double that still. I will feel as though I'm missing a huge chunk of my heart.

That's the job though, and in a strange way, that sadness is the most rewarding part. To be a part of finding them the perfect match that will not love them in spite of their quirks, but because of them. Someone who will choose them day in, day out with a certainty that just grows with every passing day. The love a dog shares with you, no matter for how brief or long, as many will know, stays in your heart forever.

I shall miss my best mate, Margate. His family will love him the longest, but I loved him first and I am so so grateful for that.

If you are interested in being the exceptionally lucky human that shares their life with this beautiful soul, then please read his profile and apply if you feel that you can give him all he needs.

Another day for Margate in kennels, after another week, another month... I'm just hoping that he doesn't have to wait until the another year mark.

Margate has been waiting for seventeen months for his ideal home to find him, that's 506 days in the same place, with people who adore him, but they're not just his people. He has to share his time with so many other dogs that are waiting for their forever homes, but none have been waiting as long as him.

He does require a special home, with people who can help him thrive and who will not fail him! But as long as they understand his needs and set him up for success, they'll get so much in return.

Margate needs a cat, child (resident and visiting) and small furry free home. He needs mental stimulation and he needs a good deal of exercise too, he is smart, so so soooo smart and this is where his humans will have to try and be smarter.

Margate learns everything very quickly and will need to have clear, set boundaries in his new home from the get-go. He will need to be given his own bed, that he learns is his bed and that he isn't allowed on the sofas or other furniture, especially to begin with. He will need a home that understands how to enrich his life, to use his brain and that LOVES a good walk. He could walk for miles. He could potentially do cani-cross as he loves to run, but Margate needs to be kept safe at all times, so he will need to be kept on a lead in anything he does (unless in a secure rented dog field area). He has a high prey drive and a nose that rivals the very best sniffer dog. If you had treats in your pocket at some point this year, he'll know.

The best set up for Margate would be a home that could adapt a room to be his area that is free of food and where he could go to sleep at night, if visitors came round or if you had to pop out to go shopping etc. He needs this to be his area that helps him feel safe and secure.

Although he can act like a big tough guy, underneath it all he is extremely sensitive and can get very worried by things.

How can we blame him for being this way? He's been let down by humans over and over again, he has had thousands of people walk by his kennel during his stay and no one chooses him.. I know that in a home that FULLY understands him and puts everything in place to set him up to succeed, then he will be the bestest boy you could ever wish to know. His heart is the size of the whole universe and he just needs someone to see him and commit to him, then they'll see what I see too. My boy, Margate, I see all of you and I'll love you like you're my own until your forever person comes along. I'll be so jealous of the person who gets to share their life with you.

Disclaimer - although he picks up agility things quite quickly, this is not something he should do to a competitive level as the environment of the events would not benefit him.

It is now over a year since Margate joined us here in Many Tears.  That’s 365 days of seeing the same kennel, and the same routine and watching his friends leave whilst he gets left behind. This year it’ll be the second Christmas in kennels and whilst others are snuggled up in a warm home and spoilt with presents, it’ll be another day in kennels for him.  Everyone here at the rescue loves Margate!

What can I say that already hasn’t been said about this wonderful boy ??

He’s a real character, smart and witty. Every morning I hear him from the staff car park and you know that he’s announcing breakfast time for the whole rescue!  At times he can be loud and bouncy but deep down he’s just a little baby who loves a good snuggle.

Margate really has improved over the year, learnt new tricks, muzzle trained, met loads of new friends and loves meeting new people! He’ll happily walk past volunteers and once instructed to sit and wait he will receive a fuss.

So far Margate has only needed the muzzle for the vets as a precaution, but he tends to turn into a puddle of mush as he’s actually quite scared of the vets.

Margate does have issues regarding high value treats, toys and beds. This is resolved by removing any food in the area, removing any toys from the yard before play time and using multiple blankets for bedding. This is something that can be discussed and helped with for potential adopters.

All Margate needs is for adopters to understand his requirement’s and who are willing to work with him in a home environment.  He is such a beautiful wonderful boy who really is wasted here in kennels?

Margate is still absolutely thriving and learning every day. He is such a clever boy who enjoys his training and the company of his kennel friend. He has come such a long way, but in a home he could truly excel as long as his new humans know how to keep his training progressing and his mind busy. I really hope he breaks free soon.

Margate is one of those dogs that once you know him, you fall head over heels in love with him. He is one of the smartest dogs I've ever worked with, picks things up incredibly quickly and tries very hard to please you. He would be the perfect only dog for someone who wants to pour lots of time in to training to build a strong bond with him and who will understand that he will train you if given a chance. He's a super special guy with so much love to give, he just needs the right person to choose him and fully commit.

Margate is a super cool bocker. He loves long walks and his time in the yard. He thrives with anything to get his brain working, so stimulation games are an excellent way of working Margate. The best way to work with him is definitely food, he will do anything for a tasty treat. Be careful though as Margate can be food possessive. As you can see by the pictures he is such an affectionate boy who loves a good fuss, to go with that he is just absolutely handsome! Margate has been in kennels for some time now and he does cope well with being in kennels, but he would just absolutely thrive in an active home with someone who is willing to keep him busy and stimulated. While he's been with us he's always had a female kennel friend but he would also be an excellent only dog, so you have the best of both worlds with Margate, but due to his exuberant nature he might grate on any resident dogs a bit . Margate is a stunning young man with bags of personality and he loves all the attention and will need an experienced owner as he is a rambunctious soul. Margate is an absolute star and deserves the best life possible, if you feel you can offer him his best life get in contact. Be sure to read all the previous updates on Margate also to get the best overall idea of this smart little man. This wonderful boy has been in kennels for far too long now and is ready to find his forever home and new loving family. Will you give him the chance to progress to the next chapter in his life?

Margate is a unique boy. Firstly, Margate will need an owner with some experience under their belt, to make sure his rehabilitation is successful. It is probably best that he is homed as an only dog, as Margate struggles to get along with some dogs due to his boisterous personality, which can be overwhelming. This doesn't mean he can't be homed with another dog, as he has had a few kennel friends at the rescue that he gets along great with, he just prefers human attention. Margate will not trust strangers 100% straight off the bat , he's always excited to see people and interact with them, but to build a bond of trust takes some time. However this is an experience that I've found extremely rewarding. I discovered this when I took him to the vets for an intimate checkup, and was surprised to discover that he would only allow me to carry out the checks. It absolutely melted my heart to know that he had complete faith in me. He really is a sweet, colourful personality with so much love and energy to give, he just needs some time, love and repetition with a regular owner to steer all that energy in a positive direction

Since his last update Margate has been enjoying learning tricks and enjoying fun little games. We have learnt to "sit" "paw" and to" speak". Margate enjoys his treats so was very keen to learn and being as smart as he is, picks up training in a flash. He is a very vocal boy and will bark until I give a treat. I do not give in and he will stop and wait patiently. Margate does need to learn manners so will need adopters to remind him of this and to show Margate right and wrong.

Margate has come on so well since being here and I must admit that I do have a soft spot for him. He enjoys his walks but does pull a little when he has found a sniff, but can easily be managed with lead training. I'm currently teaching Margate to sit when I stop walking, to get his pulling under control, and as Margate is food motivated, it’s easy to see that this can be managed with little trouble. He is already  getting the hang of it!

Margate does jump up on you and can jump quite high, so adopters will need to encourage Margate not to jump up, which I am currently showing him, but again he is a very smart boy who is eager to learn. He is a funny, friendly, and vocal boy who will needs adopters to be mindful of his needs and issues and redirect him properly for him to be the best boy he can. He is still kenneled with his friend who both still continue to share their bed and space together with no problems. He is still only young and I can't wait to see him flourish more than what kennel life can offer him. Please read Margate's profile and updates and see what an amazing boy he is. He is perfect for someone that loves to share an active lifestyle with their dog, and we can’t wait to see pictures of him on all his future adventures!

Margate is a gorgeous boy who I have spent a lot of time getting to know recently and has come along way since his last update. He greets you with a waggy tail and excitement when he knows you and does enjoy a gentle fuss. He is also getting used to his name and when you call him he will turn to you. Margate has very high energy and needs an active household who enjoys long walks and adventures and are able to stimulate him mentally as well as physically. He also walks on a lead but can pull, this can easily be managed with lead training and I believe will settle once he gets to go on regular adventures with his new family as he enjoys the sniffs of the world . Margate loves to learn and play and due to his breed, he will need adopters to understand the Bocker breed and the energy they have. Margate currently is kennelled with a female cocker and they spend their time together playing in the yard and rolling around in their beds together. They both get on very well!

Due to Margate's resource guarding, him and his kennel friend have bedding and blankets rather than plastic normal beds and they both share this with no problems. Margate does have food guarding issues  and is fed separately from his kennel friend. This can also be managed in a home with another dog if he is separated at feeding times. Margate does take treats from you when his kennel friend is present and has not shown any issues towards them but this still needs to be remembered. I have been visiting him when I'm not on the same section to issue treats around other dogs in proximity to get him used to being in the same space around other dogs with food and treats. With these small issues, Margate will need an adult home with no children.

Margate has mastered a few tricks with treats, learning to sit and give paw. He gets very excited when you ask him but need to tell him firmly. Margate can be jumpy and will jump on you so he will need a firm voice to encourage him manners. Margate learns quickly so I think teaching him house training, lead training as well as other tricks and manners will come quickly for him, especially with treats!

He is a stunning boy and overlooked here at the rescue. If you have a Bocker sized hole in your heart and are active, love long walks, adventures and fun. Please consider applying for Margate.

Hi! My name is Margate I am 2 years old male Bocker (Cocker X Beagle) and have found myself at this amazing place called Many Tears. When I first arrived, I was extremely worried with my new surroundings, and adapting back to kennel life was very tough. I have spent a little time in a home but because of my high energy levels and need for adventures my family wasn’t able to provide this so unfortunately through no fault of my own I have set out on an adventure to find my new forever home. In my new home I would need a child free home with no visiting children. In my new home I will need someone who understands my breed and have had experience with similar breeds. I am currently in a bedroom (Kennel) with a female Bocker called Nova. We don’t interact too much but we do enjoy one another’s company so I could be happy having another friend in my new home upon having a meet and greet at the rescue. I can also be an only dog as long as I have a loving owner who plays lots of games with me. I have mastered walking on a lead and I absolutely love to explore and go on walks so having a busy home who is as on the go as I am would really benefit my energy levels. I still struggle a little to find the toilet but if I’m shown I will definitely find a way to go in the right place. when I meet new people I can become a little shy and anxious so go slow with me, have a kind voice and I will wag my tail and ask you for some fuss. After spending a day with you i trust very easily and I will become your best friend. If you’d like to meet me I am currently at the many tears rescue.Margate is still waiting for his forever home at Many Tears and despite his struggles around food and resource guarding from humans and dogs when food is involved, all he needs is an experienced home with the ability to adapt and help Margate with this. He will bring so much joy into your home and he can't wait to be a cherished member of the family where he can go on lots of adventures and finally be loved.

Margate is a stunning but very confused boy who is overwhelmed to be here and has been through so much change recently. He has come to us from a breeder to find his forever home. Initially when you first meet him he is mistrustful of strangers and unsure about being handled - he will make his worries known by growling. However once you have spent a few minutes in his company showing that you are safe he quickly warms up to you and jumps all over you for love and attention. We suspect he may have lived in a home before and then returned to the breeder hence his being unsure of people initially which is fair enough, poor boy! Margate already walks on a lead and would probably prefer to be an only dog.  Margate will need a cat free home as he is far too interested in them and a home without children or visiting children as he would find them too much. Margate may need help learning to live in a home and being taught housetraining skills. Margate is such a poor soul who just wants to be loved and to feel safe. Margate would need a home that is experienced in resource guarding and can help Margate work through his struggles. Margate can resource guard when he has food or high value treats/chews/bones around both dogs and people so this would need to be managed with an environment set up to ensure lovely Margate is set up for success.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Cuba
description:
I will need a loving and understanding adult-only home!

I'd prefer to be the only dog. I can't live with cats.

I am still unsure about the world, so a secure garden would be ideal.

I walk well on lead but I can be spooked. Let's take it slow!

Please be patient with me, I'm nervous and learning everything from scratch.

I have been neutered and am ready for my forever home. Read below for more!

Cuba is a very sensitive and shy boy who is still finding the world quite overwhelming, but who is making lovely progress as his confidence slowly grows. Cuba arrived with very little life experience and can be easily worried by new situations, so he needs a calm, gentle approach and plenty of time to build trust.

As he gets to know people, Cuba is becoming increasingly sociable. He may need a couple of meetings to feel comfortable, but once he trusts you, he will happily approach with his tail wagging and enjoy quiet, gentle fuss. He particularly loves relaxing in his bed with trusted company nearby and finds calm strokes very reassuring, often drifting off to sleep while being gently fussed. As his confidence grows, his playful side is beginning to shine. He adores playing with toys, cuddling, giving kisses, and will happily bounce around like a lunatic when he’s feeling safe and excited.

Cuba has never lived in a home before, so everything will be new to him. He will need experienced, adult-only adopters who understand nervous dogs and are happy to let him settle at his own pace. Patient guidance will be needed as he learns all about home life, including house training and walking on a harness and lead. He has recently started harness training and is beginning to enjoy it, but this will still need to be continued slowly and positively, starting with gentle practice in the garden. Once out and about, Cuba enjoys taking in the world at his own pace and loves sniffing everything in sight on his walks.

Cuba would prefer to be the only dog in the home, so his adopters will need to be around enough to keep him company and provide reassurance as he settles.

He will need a home without cats or small furries as he does have a prey drive.

He is quite fond of using his voice and enjoys a good grumble rather than barking, which is very much part of his character. Beneath his worried exterior, Cuba is a sweet, gentle boy who has already formed strong bonds with familiar staff and is learning that not all people are bad.

Cuba will need a calm and understanding home where his boundaries are respected and he can have his own safe space. With kindness, patience, and the right support, he has the potential to continue blossoming into a truly rewarding companion.

Cuba has had a bilateral entropion repair.

Cubawill need more than one meet and greet with his prospective adopters to allow him time to build a bond and feel comfortable with them before being adopted.

Cuba has had quite a busy few weeks at the rescue and has had to put his big boy pants on!! I’ve started taking him into our on site cabin which is similar to being in a home environment. It’s got a sofa, bed, TV etc … so I thought it would be a good idea to start taking him in there to help desensitise him to new things.

Obviously he’s never been in a home before, so everything was completely brand new for him. The first time he was very nervous and really didn’t know what to do at all. He was holding himself low to the floor and hunched, but he was walking around doing little sniffs of things. I was sat on the sofa and he did come over to me a couple of times for reassurance which did help a little, but after about only 5 minutes I took him back to his kennel as I didn’t want to overdo it.

The second time he was still nervous again, but after a little while he did start to drop his guard and became quite curious. He kept coming over to me for a fuss and a treat, so that was a really good sign being brave enough to eat in there. He was fascinated by the TV and didn’t quite know what to think of it. He was a bit scared of it I think, but equally as intrigued. I’m going to keep taking him into the cabin once a week or so just to ease him into it slowly, and then hopefully that will help his transition to home life when he does get adopted.

Cuba has been with me for over 3 months now and I literally love him more and more every day. I’m lucky to have a very close relationship with him and he loves me and trusts me completely, so I see a side to him that not many other people get to see.

He absolutely oozes charisma and character, and he makes me laugh and smile every time we’re together. He’s fiercely loyal, super affectionate, and SO comical. He gives the best of everything. He loves running around the yard with me and playing with toys, but loves a big sit down snuggle equally as much. So much so, he now likes to sprawl across my whole lap and fall into a deep sleep on me (snores and all)! His kennel is filled with plush toys that he loves to carry around in his mouth and sleep with, and he’s SO gentle with them. Isn’t he just the sweetest boy!?

He does still find a lot of things quite challenging, but I do feel as though we’re still making steady progress, and you can’t rush certain things. He’s still very easily spooked by loud noises, cars passing, people trying to approach him etc, so on walks especially I do have to make sure I’m paying complete attention to him. Most of the time he does recover quickly with a bit of reassurance from me, but then sometimes it will ruin his whole walk and he’ll immediately want to turn around and go back to the safety of his kennel. That’s okay though - I always allow it and take him back to make sure he knows he always has a safe place as and when he needs it.

We’ve briefly mentioned in a previous update that Cuba doesn’t like other dogs that much, but I hope this won’t put you off him as he’s not reactive to other dogs out on walks or anything like that - he simply just doesn’t like to share his space with them. I don’t think he was socialised with others apart from his old kennel friend before he came to us, so I think it’s understandable.

If you have the time and the patience to put the work in with Cuba, I can promise you 10 times over that adopting him will be the best decision of your life. Once he settles, he will truly be your best friend and the love of your life. That’s how I feel about him and I’m just his kennel mum!! He is utterly special and he deserves someone equally as special, who will love him as much as he will love them. He has such a big heart and he really does deserve the world and more.

Cuba has come on leaps and bounds over the past few weeks, and I feel so lucky to have been appointed as his “Kennel Auntie,” spending two days a week with this incredibly handsome boy.

Although Cuba can still be wary of strangers, the bond he forms once he trusts you is truly something special. Knowing him from when he first arrived, you honestly wouldn’t recognise him now. He used to hide away at the back of his kennel, wishing we would go away, now he demands to be the centre of attention and absolutely smothers you in kisses. While he may take a little time to warm up to new people, once he feels safe, his affectionate nature shines through.

Cuba has taken some big steps (quite literally!) and is absolutely smashing his harness training. His progress has been incredible, and his confidence grows more each day. He happily trots along on walks, tail wagging, taking in all the exciting new smells he’s never experienced before. Watching him discover so many “firsts” is truly magical.

We’ve found that Cuba isn’t too fussed about other dogs (aside from his kennel friend Chippy), so he would be best suited to a home where he can be the only dog. This will allow him to soak up all the love and attention he thrives on.

I cherish every moment I get to spend with Cuba. He is the sweetest, soppiest boy. He is so full of love, yet not enough people get to see this side of him. He adores playing with toys, cuddling, bouncing around like a lunatic, giving kisses, and sniffing everything in sight on his walks. But most of all, he loves his people.

There isn’t a moment with Cuba where you won’t feel loved. He makes every second count and whoever is lucky enough to adopt him should do the same. He truly is a special boy, and you wouldn’t want to miss out on him.

Cuba has been on my section for a few weeks now and I am just head over heels in love with him. I knew he was a nervous and sensitive boy so I have taken things slow with him and left him be to suss me out and get used to my routine, but it really didn’t take long for him to become quite attached to me. Within a few days he started to trust me, and now I would say he actually trusts me completely. So much so, I’ve been able to start harness training with him, put medication in his eyes after his surgery, and he’s given me his belly a couple of times for fusses!!!

I still haven’t taken him outside of the secure yard on his harness yet, but I do think he’s ready so I’m going to try it really soon. His biggest fear was having any tension on the lead - he would get really spooked by it and panic - but I think we’re just about over that now and he’s gotten used to it.With Cuba, once he gets to know you and forms a bond with you he is an extremely affectionate, happy, and playful boy, but he is still standoffish with people he doesn’t know and he doesn’t trust them to touch him or handle him. This is something his future adopters will need to take into consideration and prepare for. He’ll just need to be given some time, patience and gentleness to begin with. I am certain he’ll come around, but it will need to be in his own time as he can’t be pushed.

Cuba is hilarious and so goofy, and he will make such a great addition to someone’s home in time, but it does need to be the right home to ensure he’s set up for only success. I adore him very much and I just know his future parents will too, so please keep him in mind if you think you could be a good match for him!

Cuba is making lovely progress and is becoming increasingly sociable as his confidence grows. He is still finding walking on a harness quite scary and will need a patient home willing to help him continue this training at his own pace.

Cuba may need a couple of meetings to feel comfortable, but once he knows you, he will happily approach with his tail wagging and enjoy a gentle fuss. He particularly loves relaxing in his bed with trusted company nearby, quietly enjoying being stroked as he drifts off to sleep.

Cuba prefers to have his own space at mealtimes, as he can be a little protective over his food while eating, and this should be respected in his new home.

At heart, he is a very sweet and gentle boy who is learning to trust and has already formed strong bonds with familiar staff. He is simply a scared boy who needs a fresh start in a calm, understanding and respectful home where he can continue to blossom.

Cuba is a very nervous and shy boy who is finding the world quite overwhelming at the moment. He needs a slow, gentle approach and plenty of time to build trust, but once he feels safe with someone, he will allow calm, gentle fuss while settled in his bed, which he finds very reassuring. Underneath his worried exterior, Cuba is simply a big, scared baby who needs kindness, patience, and understanding to help him feel secure.

Cuba will need an adult-only home with experienced adopters who understand nervous dogs and are happy to let him settle at his own pace. He has never lived in a home before, so everything will be completely new to him. His adopters will need to be patient and supportive as he learns about home life, including house training and how to walk on a harness and lead. Gentle practice in the garden will be an important starting point before he is ready to venture further afield.

Cuba will also need at least one kind, confident resident dog in the home. He gains comfort and confidence from other dogs and will benefit greatly from having a calm canine role model to help guide him and show him that the world is a safe place. He is not suitable to be an only dog.

Cuba gets fed separately from his kennel friends as he can be a little protective of his food from other dogs while eating. That said, he is fine taking treats when around other dogs, but would need to be fed separately at meal times or when having long lasting treats and chews in his new home.

Cuba is currently learning to walk on a harness, but this is something he finds very challenging and will require a great deal of time, patience, and dedication. He is also quite fond of using his voice and enjoys a good grumble rather than barking, which is very much part of his character.

Cuba will need a calm, quiet home where he can have his own safe space and plenty of downtime alongside gentle interaction. With love, reassurance, and understanding adopters, he will slowly begin to realise that not all people are bad and that he is finally safe. In the right home, with the right support, Cuba has the potential to blossom into a deeply rewarding companion.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

name: Primrose
description:
I am looking for an incredibly special, adult only home.

I need at least one other dog in my new home who is happy to share their bed.

I need a very secure garden in my new home.

I am not ready to learn about walking on a lead and harness yet.

I am in a foster home and am currently using puppy pads.

I am spayed and ready to find my forever home.

Primrose is a very scared little girl who has come to us from a breeder to start her new life. When she first arrived, she was completely shut down and was too fearful to even leave her bed, choosing to cuddle close to her kennel friends. She had clearly never experienced much in life before and the world around her felt overwhelming and frightening. She soon moved to a wonderful foster home, where she has spent the last several months settling in and adjusting to home life.

Primrose is a very special little dog who will need an equally special home. When she first arrived in foster, she was utterly petrified of people. For several weeks she would bury her head into her chest and hide under a cupboard just to avoid looking at her foster carer. Although she is still very fearful of humans, she has come a long way since then. She doesn’t interact with people or other dogs, but she does find enormous comfort in calm, affectionate little dogs who choose to cuddle into her.

In foster, Primrose has slowly begun to blossom in her own quiet way. She now chooses to sit in one of her three “safe places,” finding comfort in having spaces that feel secure and predictable. She has started to trust her foster family and looks to her fosterer for treats and is now even happy to be placed on the sofa next to her and on occasion has even rested her head on her lap, which is a huge step for Primrose.

At night, when the home is quiet and everyone is in bed, Primrose starts to come out of her shell and has even been seen on camera emptying the toy box and playing by herself, which is a lovely glimpse of the happy dog she may become once she feels truly safe.

Primrose is still incredibly frightened of anything beyond her safe spaces, but is now venturing outdoors into the garden. Her foster mum does have to put her outside, but when she does she is happy to explore it and runs around with excitement. She’s even played chase a couple of times with her foster brothers and sisters. Whilst incredibly nervous she is clearly coming out of her shell a little more now, and her foster mum says it’s wonderful to see this most gentle dog, finally learn it’s okay to be happy and have fun.

She is now happy to wear a harness, although she is not yet ready for walks. She is currently using puppy pads for her toileting.

Primrose will be looking for a calm and quiet, adult-only home with adopters who have experience with scared ex-breeding dogs and who will understand that she needs to be allowed to settle in and progress at her own pace, with no pressure or expectations.

She will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog who will take her under their wing, cuddle up with her and offer her the reassurance she needs as she settles in to her new home. She seems to prefer smaller dogs and dogs around her size and is less comfortable around larger dogs.

Her adopters must be prepared to dedicate the time, patience, and kindness needed to help her continue building confidence. A quiet environment with no visiting young children is essential. Her fosterer will support the adopter through this process.

With patience, understanding, and love, Primrose has the potential to blossom fully and show the happy, affectionate dog she should have always had the chance to be. She just needs the right family to help her take the next steps.

Primrose has been given the Gift of Life by an Anonymous Supporter

Primrose is a very special little dog who will need a very special home.

She is an incredibly frightened ex-breeding dog whose past has clearly left her deeply traumatised. When she first arrived in foster five months ago, she was utterly petrified of people. For several weeks she would bury her head into her chest and hide under a cupboard just to avoid looking at her foster carer. Although she is still very fearful of humans, she has come a long way since then.

Primrose is probably the gentlest dog her foster carer has ever looked after, but also the most emotionally damaged. She is sweet, patient, and completely unassuming. She never causes any trouble, joins in with nothing, and asks for very little. She doesn’t interact with people or other dogs, but she does find enormous comfort in calm, affectionate little dogs who choose to cuddle into her. She seems to especially like small dogs and appears less comfortable around larger ones.

In foster, Primrose’s little canine housemates have gently helped her in their own way. They share her bed, steal her treats, pinch her food, take her spot on the sofa, and she accepts it all with quiet patience. If placed on the sofa or bed, she will happily snuggle down beside them. She does not actively seek dogs out for play or socialising, but she clearly takes comfort from being close to kind, cuddly companions.

Primrose spends most of her time in her safe places and will usually only move from one safe space to another if encouraged. She does not yet venture out confidently on her own. However, at night, when the house is quiet and her foster carer has gone to bed, Primrose becomes a different little soul. Caught on camera, she carefully empties every toy out of the toy box, and plays by herself. It is a lovely glimpse of the happy dog she may become once she feels truly safe.

Over the past month, Primrose has started to show small but meaningful signs of progress. She now looks to her foster carer for treats, especially when sitting together on the sofa, and she has rested her head on her a couple of times. These moments may seem tiny, but for Primrose they are huge steps forward.

Primrose is currently using puppy pads, as she is still too frightened to go outdoors of her own accord. When placed outside, she will usually tuck herself into a corner of the garden. More recently, if carried around the garden, she has started to show some interest in her surroundings. She is now happy to wear a harness, although she is not yet ready for walks.

Primrose will need an exceptionally kind, patient, and understanding adopter with experience of ex-breeding dogs or very fearful dogs. She must be allowed to go at her own pace, with no pressure and no expectations of quick progress. She needs a calm home with at least one small, affectionate dog who loves to cuddle, as canine companionship is essential for her confidence and comfort.

She also seems wary of men, particularly men with beards, and appears to feel safer with women.

Primrose is both the sweetest and the most broken little dog her foster carer has ever known. But after five months, she is slowly starting to come alive. She needs someone who can see the beauty in the smallest steps and who will cherish every tiny piece of trust she gives. For the right person, helping Primrose heal will be an extraordinary privilege.

Given the chance, Primrose will choose to sit on her own in one of her three “safe places” in her foster home. Whilst it is common for ex-breeding dogs that have never lived in a home before to not want to be near humans, Primrose unusually chooses not to be with the other dogs living in the house either.Whilst she has progressed significantly since going into care, we have decided that there is still a little way to go before we make her available for adoption.Primrose flees from one of her “safe places” to another keeping her body close to the ground. Her foster mum wants to see her walking upright as well as expose her to areas beyond her comfort zones, including outdoors which she currently fears, so she is better prepared for adoption.Whilst her progress is slow, she has still come along way from the early days when she would bury her head in her chest or hide behind or under furniture. She’s even played with a ball for about a minute, which her fosterer said was possibly the most moving experience she’s seen with a dog in years, suggesting there is a happy dog “inside” Primrose wanting to come out.She also uses a puppy pad if placed close to her bed. She eats well, is very gentle, and allows her quieter foster brothers and sisters to cuddle up to her. She also likes her tummy rubbed, is happy to be carried for short periods, and if put next to her foster mum, will lay down and sleep.Before Primrose will be made available for adoption we would like to see Primrose walking upright as well as walking outdoors which she still fears.Anyone applying to adopt Primrose will need experience with very frightened ex-breeding dogs that have never lived in a home. They will need to have at least one confident gentle dog,  ideally no larger than her, and a quiet home with no visiting young children. Importantly they will need to be prepared to dedicate the time it will take to help develop Primrose’s confidence, Her fosterer is happy to help guide the adopter going forwards.

Primrose is a very scared little girl and is unsure of her new surroundings. She hasn’t yet found the courage to leave her bed but she finds comfort in cuddling close to her kennel friends because that’s the only comfort she’s ever known.

Primrose needs a calm, adult only home with at least one gentle dog to guide her and give her the reassurance she needs and who will allow her to cuddle up to them for support and comfort if she wants to. She has never lived in a home, so everything will feel overwhelming at first. She will have no idea about house training and the garden may be as scary as being in the home at first so she will need adopters who have plenty of time and patience to help her.

Lead walking will be a new experience for her and should start in the garden before venturing into the outside world.

With endless patience, kindness and time, her new family will see Primrose slowly open up and blossom into the happy girl she was always meant to be.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Gala
description:
I need a quiet adult-only home with no visiting kids to keep me stress-free.

I need at least one resident dog to help me feel safe, preferably a female my size.

I’m frightened and will need a secure garden to learn about being outside.

I enjoy secure enclosed areas where I can run around, but the lead still worries me.

I’m learning to walk on the lead, it's scary for me but I am toilet trained!

I’m fully vaccinated and spayed, ready for my forever home!

Gala is an extremely sweet but incredibly sensitive girl who originally came to us from a breeder. Sadly, she has already been adopted twice and returned both times, as the homes simply weren’t the right fit for her. Because of this, Gala is now searching for a very special forever home with people who truly understand her needs and are committed to giving her the time, patience, and stability she has never had.

When she first arrived, she was an extremely scared, flighty, and shut-down girl who wanted nothing to do with us humans. Since then, Gala has made some lovely, if small, steps forward. She will now allow a gentle stroke while she is on the sofa, usually for around a minute before she chooses to move away. This is huge progress for her and shows just how brave she is becoming.

On one occasion, she even chose to jump up onto the sofa and sit beside someone. Although she ran off as soon as there was a slight movement, it was still a big milestone and a really positive sign of her growing confidence.

That said, Gala is still a very worried girl who carries a lot of fear, and she has a long way to go. She is still frightened of interaction and will run away when approached. However, once she has settled into one of her beds, she will stay still to be gently picked up or have her lead put on.

She remains very nervous of the harness and lead. While she settles a little once it is on, the process of putting it on is still very scary for her. This will need to be built up slowly and gently to avoid overwhelming her or setting her back. She does know how to walk on a lead, but finds it quite daunting, and the outside world is still very overwhelming. She will need time and patience, starting in a secure garden to help build her confidence before any bigger adventures.

Gala takes great comfort from other dogs and truly thrives in their company. She loves to snuggle up with them and will be happiest in a home with other female dogs — the more, the better if possible. She has already shown that she forms particularly strong bonds, often choosing a favourite companion and looking to them for guidance. A kind, confident, and playful female dog of a similar size would be ideal for her, as she finds reassurance and happiness with other dogs while humans are still a source of uncertainty.

She also has a lovely playful side beginning to emerge, and she enjoys carrying around and playing with cuddly toys, which seem to bring her comfort and contentment.

Gala cannot live with cats and will need a calm, child-free home, including no visiting children, where she can feel safe and secure.

Gala has never truly known security, so what she needs most now is patience, kindness, and someone who will never give up on her. There won’t be any overnight miracles, and she may never become a confident, outgoing dog, but with the right person by her side, we know she can continue to grow in confidence, heal from her past, and finally understand what it means to feel safe and loved.

Gala has visited the vets regarding a small mass of inguinal fat. This is not causing her any harm and is currently being left alone, but it may need monitoring in the future.

Gala has made a few small improvements over the past month. She will now allow us to give her a short stroke while she’s on the sofa, usually for about a minute before she moves away. This is massive progress for her and shows how brave she’s becoming.

On one occasion, she even jumped up onto the sofa and sat beside me. As soon as I moved slightly, she ran off, but it was still a big step forward and she did so well.

She is still very scared of the harness. Once it’s on, she settles a little, but the process of putting the lead on frightens her. It’s going to be a very slow process, as we don’t want to push her too far and risk setting her back.

She also loves carrying around and playing with a cuddly toy, which seems to make her feel more content and happy.

Gala is still happiest when surrounded by other female dogs — the more, the better if possible. She has chosen a favourite and looks to her for guidance. A confident, playful female companion would be ideal for her, as she finds comfort in other dogs while humans are still a source of fear.

Gala has been back in foster for a few weeks and she seems to be happy and loves being with her dog friends. She settles very quickly and will happily sleep on a comfy chair. But she is still a very worried girl, as although looking settled, If one of us gets up, she'll be up and running to hide out of our  way. We have found routine is the best thing for her, she is more confident with this.

She very quickly learns what to do and does get animated when certain times arrive, such as breakfast and dinner - as long as she doesn't need to be too near a human. She will definitely need a very stable home without changes and without expectation. At the moment she will interact with us from a distance ok but doesn't get close. If you approach slowly she will stay put and you can stroke her or pick her up but she's not comfortable with it and would much rather escape from you...

On the positive side, she has started to show signs of enjoying a tummy rub, she will lift her leg for more to show she's liking it - almost like her doggy instincts are getting the better of her, this is the only time she happily excepts touching that she seems to enjoy.

Putting her harness on she shakes with fear when she sees her harness and it needs putting on very very slowly. She then is happy to walk in her own surroundings on a lead, but is so scared of being outside in the big world on it and will try to escape.

However she does love being taken to a secure field where she can run around playing with her furry friends. She loves all our other dogs and interacts very well with them all. Plus she is house trained.

Her new home must be one who gives her patience and time and expects nothing in return, this could be for a long time. She needs a very stable quiet home without too many changes happening and a good routine She wouldn't react well to things like doggy day care or holidays. She needs some very gentle persuasion to gain trust on humans but without expectations so she is not pushed too far and regressed.

Gala has been back at the rescue with me for just about two months now. I also looked after her when she was at the rescue originally, around a year and a half ago. Seeing her arrive back was hard for me, but it was nice to see how much progress she’s made during her time away. When I knew her all that time ago, she was an extremely scared, flighty, and shut-down girl who wanted nothing to do with us humans. It’s very clear she has gained confidence since then and has (sort of) learnt that she can trust us humans, so it is lovely to see the difference in her. Having said that, she does still carry a lot of worry and fear, and she still has a long way to go. Steps have been made in the right direction, so hopefully she can continue learning and growing, and eventually try to put her past behind her.Gala’s biggest love is food, so this is the main thing I’ve been using to get her comfortable around me. She has learnt how to ‘sit’ and also how to give the best puppy dog eyes that I can never say no to! She waits patiently for her turn for a treat, and takes them nice and gently too.Gala does still have quite a big fear of human hands and won’t approach me for a fuss, but she is good when it comes to things such as being groomed. When I need to pick her up, she’ll usually jump into one of her beds and then stay still for me to scoop her. She knows how to walk on a harness, but the outside world is another one of her fears, so this is something that needs to be taken slowly with her. I doubt very much she’ll be up for big adventures any time soon and will need a lot of practice in the garden and very quiet areas for a good while, so this is something her future adopters will need to take into consideration.After a life full of uncertainty and confusion, her next home HAS to be the right one for her. She really needs adopters who will love her regardless of her worries and work with her through them. Most importantly of all, she can’t be given up on again. She deserves to know where she’s going to wake up in the morning and not have to worry about being passed from pillar to post. She deserves love, trust, and commitment … and not a smidge less.

Sadly, Gala has returned to the kennels after her recent adoption. Although her new family cared deeply for her, it turned out not to be the right match. This decision was made with everyone's best interests at heart, and she’s now back with us while we look for her perfect forever home. Gala is a wonderful dog who just needs the right environment to truly thrive, and we’re hopeful that her special someone is out there waiting for her.

Gala is still in foster and gradually making progress. She is still extremely hand-shy but has started to come up and let her nose touch your hand before running off! She will then come back wanting to do it again and wagging her tail (huge progress!).Gala is looking for a forever home to offer her patience and lots of time to help her get over her fears. She is fully house trained and very happy to follow our other dogs around copying what they do. She is an escape artist on a harness as she is still nervous walking on a lead, but we have found taking her to a secure dog run field is amazing for her and she absolutely loves her freedom running around. Gala is ideally looking for some playmates in her new home as she does love to run and play with other dogs.

Gala is doing brilliantly in her foster home. She loves the company of other dogs and loves to play with them but is still very hand-shy with us humans and very wary.Gala would love a home with one or more furry friends who love playing, but what she also needs is a home which would help her understand to not be frightened anymore and people who will understand it's going to take time and patience until you get any affection or love back from her.Gala has big trust issues and needs a selfless home to get her over this hurdle she is facing.

Gala has sadly returned to the kennels through no fault of her own after a six month adoption. Her adopter made the difficult decision to bring her back due to a change in their personal circumstances, and she was much loved during her time with them. She’s now back with us and receiving lots of care and attention while we help her find another loving home. Gala is a lovely dog who truly deserves her second chance.

Gala came to us from a breeder a few months ago. We are told she is a Poodle cross Cavalier cross Bichon and she is the cutest little girl but is very overwhelmed and nervous so does need an experienced home who have had scared ex-breeding dogs before.Sadly Gala was returned after a short adoption due to a change in her adopters circumstances but was lucky enough to go to a foster home. She will need a calm and quiet home and is very scared of children so will need an adult only home with no visiting children. She will need a secure garden as she is so worried she can be a flight risk.Gala will need a home with at least one other kind and confident resident dog to be her friend and help her to settle in. She is house trained, but may need some reminders in her new home and walking on a lead is a little scary for her, so she will need more practice with this. She LOVES food and that's the way to her heart, she will take a treat from our hand, but other than that she is very hand shy and will run away if we go to stroke or approach her.This little lady just needs to find her perfect home for she can learn that people can offer her kindness and love. She is a sweet girl who with time, love and kindness will soon start to blossom.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Acorn
description:
This tiny little Acorn has a big personality. What he lacks in size, he makes up for in his cheeky ways. It’s never a dull moment when he’s around.

This little chap is looking for someone who will teach him the ways of the world and will see more than just his cuteness – Acorn will need time, patience and training to help him flourish.

Support and Health:
How much training will I need? Needs ongoing support
Can I be left alone? No - Acorn finds being left at home on his own a struggle and will need a dedicated new family who can work on this training.
Do I have any medical conditions? Existing conditions - Acorn has had a dental to remove some of his baby teeth that hadn’t fallen out and may require another dental in the future. We would also advise that any medical conditions, be that historic or ongoing, are discussed with your own vet prior to taking them home. Due to the nature of the medical condition, a vet call with one of our onsite vets will be required before the pet goes home.

Who I can live with:
Can I live with dogs? Yes
Can I live with cats? Yes, conditionally - Acorn has the potential to live with cats, pending steady and appropriate introductions.
Can I live with small pets? Yes, conditionally - Acorn is able to live with securely housed small pets, that he won't have access too.
Additional information - Acorn would enjoy living with another small – medium size dog that will be respectful and careful of his tiny 1.6kg body!

Family and environment:
What kind of family am I looking for? - Acorn is looking for a home with children aged 11 or over, as he can be uncomfortable with certain types of handling and around food. Although he’s a tiny boy, he appreciates having his personal space respected and thrives when his body language is understood and listened to.
What type of environment would suit me? - Acorn needs a secure, private garden to help with his toilet training and to run around and play in.

name: Ben
description:
Meet Ben! This sweet boy is looking for a retirement home with a sofa to snooze on and a family to give lots of cuddles to. He is a very relaxed gentleman and like to take life at his own pace. He enjoys his walks and likes to play with toys but 90% of the time he is snoozing in his blankets!

Ben is a very affectionate boy who loves people and is always happy to have a cuddle.If you are looking for a chilled companion to give you lots of love, then Ben could be the dog for you!

Support and Health:
How much training will I need? Needs some support
Can I be left alone? Potentially, once settled - Ben has the potential to be left home alone for a few hours once he settles into his new home. Any time will need to be built up gradually, to ensure he is comfortable with it.
Do I have any medical conditions? Existing conditions - Benhas a current medical condition namely some flaky skin, which he is on some soothing baths for, that will require consideration before this pet is rehomed. Our rehoming team will be able to give you further information if you apply to rehome them and you are a successful match. We would also advise that any medical conditions, be that historic or ongoing, are discussed with your own vet prior to taking them home. Due to the nature of the medical condition, a vet call with one of our onsite vets will berequiredbefore the pet goes home.

Who I can live with:
Can I live with dogs? Yes
Can I live with cats? No
Can I live with small pets? No
Additional information - Ben could happily live with another dog who is medium sized and up. He is a calm boy who doesn’t really understand how to play currently, so any resident dogs will need to be able to match his chilled out lifestyle.

Family and environment:
What kind of family am I looking for? - Ben is a friendly and gentle boy, who can live with children aged 8 plus.
What type of environment would suit me? - Ben is looking for a secure, private garden to sunbathe in and stretch his long legs! He does require 6ft fencing all around.

name: Blu and Terry
description:
Meet Terry and Blu — a wonderful duo with so much love to give.

Blu is not only Terry’s best friend but also his loyal guide dog. Together, they make an inseparable team. Terry doesn’t need his eyes to see the world — his incredible senses tell him everything he needs to know. With Blu by his side, he navigates life with confidence, curiosity, and plenty of personality. He loves nothing more than sniffing around, exploring his surroundings, and being close to his people.

Blu also loves being out exploring, enjoying some play and then soaking in all the love.

Together, Terry and Blu are a truly special pair, full of love, trust, and companionship.

Support and Health:
How much training will I need? Needs some support - Blu and Terry are best friends, can you offer them the forever home they need?
Can I be left alone? Yes - Terry and Blue have been able to be left comfortably for 3 hours in their foster home. Any home alone time will need to be refreshed in their new home but they should be able to do a few hours again.
Do I have any medical conditions? Existing conditions - Terry has historic medical conditions, namely blindness; he has also had episodes of coughing and a cyst that was removed, which have since resolved. Blu has historic medical conditions, namely having Brachycephalic Airway Obstruction Syndrome (BOAS) Grade 1; this does not currently require treatment. She has also had an ear infection, an ulcer on the eye, and a benign mass that have both been removed and are now resolved. Our rehoming team will be able to give you further information if you apply to rehome them and you are a successful match. We would also advise that any medical conditions, be that historic or ongoing, are discussed with your own vet prior to taking them home.

Who I can live with:
Can I live with dogs? No
Can I live with cats? Yes, conditionally - A dog savvy, bold cat following correct introductions
Can I live with small pets? Yes, conditionally - Securely housed small pets
Additional information - Blu and Terry must be rehomed together; they’re best buddies. Terry gets excited when he hears other dogs on his walks. Blu isn’t very interested in other dogs but is happy to say a quick hello before moving on. Terry and Blu are happy to have some calm walking buddies, who, like them, are happy to enjoy the sights and smells of their walk rather than a playmate.

Family and environment:
What kind of family am I looking for? - Terry and Blu can live with children aged 8+ that can respect and understand that Terry is blind. They are an affectionate pair who love being around their favourite people. When Terry hears you he will run over for some fuss and relies on his nose to get around.
What type of environment would suit me? - Terry and Blu will need to have a secure private garden of their own. Due to Terry being completely blind this will be a nice safe space for him to explore.

name: Charlie and Loki
description:
Meet Charlie & Loki – A Bonded Pair Looking for Their Forever Home

Charlie and Loki are two loving brothers searching for a special home where they can stay together. These handsome boys share an incredibly close bond and do everything side by side — including curling up together in the same bed at the end of the day.

Both dogs adore people and are wonderfully affectionate once they know you. They love fuss, attention, and being close to their humans, making them fantastic companions for someone looking for loyal and loving dogs.

Charlie and Loki enjoy their walks, especially in quieter areas where they can relax and explore with no other dogs around. They would thrive in a calm environment with owners who understand that while they are sweet-natured boys, they still need some ongoing training and guidance to help with dog reactivity and handling in the vets.With patience, love, and consistency, Charlie and Loki will make incredibly rewarding companions for somebody. If you’re looking for a pair of affectionate best friends to share your life with, these two could be the perfect match.

Support and Health:
How much training will I need? Needs ongoing support
Can I be left alone? Potentially, once settled - Charlie and Loki were not left alone very often in their previous home. They have managed up to 4 hours before and were said to be settled. They need time to adjust to a new environment before home alone time is built up.
Do I have any medical conditions? Existing conditions - Charliehas current medical conditions, namely Obesity.Loki has current medical conditions, namely Obesity, Hypersensitivity and Diarrhoea, as well as historic Lipoma mass removal.  These will require consideration before this pet is rehomed. Our rehoming team will be able to give you further information if you apply to rehome them and you are a successful match. We would also advise that any medical conditions, be that historic or ongoing, are discussed with your own vet prior to taking them home.

Who I can live with:
Can I live with dogs? No
Can I live with cats? No
Can I live with small pets? No
Additional information - Charlie and Loki are more than happy just in the company of each other and their people.

Family and environment:
